description: This course introduces a search pattern, basic patterns of disease, and includes >110 essential real-world Chest Xray cases that cover the most important things you need to know. This includes the common and rare but important pathologies, hand picked to teach you concepts. Cases are a single worklist to avoid giving away where to look based on 'section'. I suggest working through these cases in order.

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus PA inspiration

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Multiple bilateral circular lucencies of varying diameter with variably thick walls consistent with cystic bronchiectasis. These are also seen on the lateral Xray

IMPRESSION:

Severe bronchiectasis. No obvious bronchopneumonia.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Bronchiectasis is permanent abnormal dilatation of bronchi, often with associated wall thickening and impaired mucociliary clearance.

The cause is recurrent infection (including TB, and in CF patients) or inflammation (asthma, COPD).

Chest Xray is very insensitive for bronchiectasis (will not see unless severe). CT is best for identifying bronchiectasis and categorizing into one of three types:

  • Cylindrical - most common (uniform dilatation)
  • Varicose - beaded appearance (rare)
  • Cystic - severe, cyst-like bronchi +/- air fluid levels

CXR Findings:

When visible on Xray, may see:

  • Tram-track opacities = parallel linear densities due to thickened walls
  • Ring shadows = circular / oval lucencies with thick walls (seeing bronchi en-face / end on).
  • With severe cystic bronchiectasis, may see larger cysts with variable wall thickness (like our case) +/- air-fluid levels.
  • If mucus impaction, can see tubular opacities (finger-in-glove), covered in other cases

Generally, findings on CXR are nonspecific, and CT better identifies the bronchiectasis.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Enlarged cardiomediastinal silhouette.
  • LVAD (Left Ventricular Assist Device) in situ.

Other Findings:

  • No pulmonary edema
  • No nodules or consolidation
  • No pneumothorax or pleural effusion.
  • Sternotomy wires intact.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD) is a mechanical pump that enhances cardiac output in advanced heart failure. It is placed as a bridge to transplant or therapy.

Appearance on CXR varies, but ALL have:

  • Inflow cannula at LV Apex
  • A Pump
  • Outlet graft toward ascending aorta
  • External driveline (externalized power/data cable, tunneled through abdominal wall)

CXR is performed to assess position / lines.

Devices:

Old devices (first generation) had pulsatile flow. Newer devices pump continuously, including second gen like HeartMate 2 [axial flow pump]; and third gen like HeartMate 3 and HeartWare HVAD [centrifugal pump, as shown here]. Specific devices are beyond the scope here.

LVAD Complications:

  • Malposition / Migration (compare to prior)
  • Hemothorax, Hemopericardium and tamponade, Mediastinal Hematoma (all better on CT)
  • Driveline infection (CT to assess along driveline in abdominal wall)
  • Thrombosis (CT)
  • Outflow graft kinking / compression

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tubular opacitiy in the right upper lobe, with a branching appearance (window) - giving a finger-in-glove appearance.

Other Findings:

  • Mild atelectasis at the lung bases
  • Small left pleural effusion.

Findings likely relate to bronchiectasis and mucus plugging. CT should be performed to assess for obstructing bronchial lesion.


CT was performed and demonstrated an endobronchial mass with mucus plugging downstream, giving a finger-in-glove appearance. This was confirmed to be endobronchial carcinoid tumor.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced finger-in-glove in a previous case. This case was due to an obstructing bronchial tumor (carcinoid). We introduce endobronchial tumors below.


Endobronchial tumors are lesions arising within the endobronchial lumen -- including benign lesions (e.g. hamartoma), and malignant ones (e.g. carcinoid, squamous cell, etc).

They often present with airway obstruction -- cough, wheeze, hemoptysis, recurrent infections.

CXR may show:

  • Atelectasis or complete lobar collapse (i.e. recall prior cases of lung collapse)
  • Post-obstructive pneumonia (often recurrent infection)
  • Air trapping / hyperlucency
  • Perihilar or bronchial mass / nodule
  • Mucoid impaction (finger-in-glove) - not very common (but shown in this case)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Calcified nodule in the left upper lobe with calcified left mediastinal and hilar nodes, in keeping with a Ranke Complex from previous tuberculosis.
  • The lung and pleural surfaces are clear. The bony thorax is intact.

No acute findings.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced pulmonary TB separately. When infection is walled off, it can form granulomas, which can eventually calcify. There is some terminology worth knowing here:

  • Ghon Focus = initial site of parenchymal infection in primary TB. Initial tuberculous granulomas are non-calcified, but can later calcify (calcified granuloma, visible on Xray).
  • Ghon Complex = Ghon Focus + Ipsilateral Lymphadenopathy, non-calcified. A calcified ghon complex = "Ranke Complex" (this case) which can be seen on Xray like in our case. A Ranke Complex is the natural progression (calcification) of a Ghon Complex, indicating healed primary TB.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Collapse of the right middle lobe.

  • Notice there is silhouetting of the right heart border (i.e. loss of normal right heart border) and increased opacity of the RML due to collapse/consolidation (arrows); notice the classic appearance on the lateral as well with wedge shaped opacity overlying the heart

Other Findings:

  • The left lung is emphysematous (hyperlucent, i.e. long arrow) but clear.
  • No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
  • Aortic calcifications (short arrows on frontal and lateral)

IMPRESSION:

Right middle lobe collapse and consolidation. A chest CT should be considered to assess for malignancy.


A subsequent CT confirmed malignancy as the cause.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
There are a few fundamentals to review here: silhouette sign, lobe collapse, and typical appearance of RML collapse.

Silhouette Sign:

  • Increased opacity in lung that is touching a normal border (e.g. right heart border) will result in loss of that normal border seen on CXR (i.e. obscured).
  • Loss of the normal border is referred to as "silhouette sign".
  • The principle is that loss of a normal border indicates the location of the pathology. For instance, loss of right heart border suggests a middle lobe process (RML contacts the right heart border).
  • In this case, there is loss of the normal right heart border due to atelectasis/collapse of the right middle lobe (the RML normally contacts the right heart border)

Other Findings in RML Collapse:

  • Lateral view: Triangular/wedge opacity over the heart, between the fissures, pointing toward the hilum (very helpful for confirmation, as in this case).
  • The major and minor fissures collapse towards one another, hence the sharper triangular shape (more acute angle) on the lateral VS a RML pneumonia / typical consolidation.

Cause and Next Steps?

  • Most often caused by a blocked airway, causing lung to deflate
  • Consider mucus plug or inflammation if acute/abrupt, inflammation or foreign body. Consider obstructing tumor.
  • Correlate with symptoms (cough, wheeze, fever). Think mucus plug or infection first, especially if acute / abrupt (i.e. not present on yesterday's Xray). Think tumor if persistent or in high-risk outpatients.

In this case, the patient was high risk and CT confirmed lung cancer as the cause.


Atelectasis vs Collapse:

Both refer to the same thing, but often used slightly differently

  • Atelectasis = broader term that is most often used for very small parts of the lung that are not expanded (i.e. subsegmental). Still correct to use when larger amount of lung though.
  • Collapse usually reserved for larger portion of lung (i.e. lobe collapse)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Free air under the right hemidiaphragm (linear lucency under right diaphragm here on frontal and lateral)

Other Findings:

  • Calcification at the aortic arch.
  • Minimal linear atelectasis at the left lung base.

IMPRESSION:

Free intraperitoneal gas, suggesting hollow viscus perforation.


In the absence of known recent surgery, findings should be considered perforated viscus until proven otherwise. You should call the referring team directly.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Free air tends to rise -- and so will be non-dependent. On upright films, this involves free gas collecting under the right diaphragm. Supine films are much less sensitive, as gas accumulates anteriorly which is harder to see on Xray. We cover this in greater detail in the Abdominal Xray course.


Ddx: Gas Where it Shouldn't Be

  • Gas from outside (i.e. surgery, intervention, etc)
  • Gas from inside (i.e. hollow viscus perforation)
  • Gas from infection (rare in the setting of intraperitoneal gas)


One mimic worth knowing about is interposed colon between liver and diaphragm. This will look like colon -- i.e. look for haustral folds and bowel signature. CT can be performed if in doubt.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderately extensive and diffuse interstitial (too many lines) and alveolar (patchy, more confluent) opacities, were new compared to a recent prior.
  • No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
  • Heart is normal size.

IMPRESSION:

This most likely represents noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, may represent ARDS in the appropriate clinical setting. Infection felt less likely due to rapid appearance.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Noncardiogenic pulmonary edema refers to pulmonary edema not caused by LV dysfunction. Edema occurs due to increased permeability at alveolar-capillary barrier due to inflammation / injury.

List of causes is long, but most common include ARDS (acute respiratory distress syndrome), inhalational injury, drug-induced, and transfusion-related lung injury (TRALI).

On imaging, there is interstitial and alveolar edema. The appearance is nonspecific has considerable overlap with other entities (i.e. infection, hemorrhage, etc). The short term development of diffuse changes can raise the possibility, as it did in this case.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Resection of the left first rib (no left first rib), with some subQ emphysema due to recent surgery
  • There is a small left apical pneumothorax (see pleural line)
  • Small to moderate left pleural effusion (not elevated diaphragm - notice silhouetting of heart border, stomach bubble is far inferior, etc)
  • Passive atelectasis adjacent to effusion

IMPRESSION:

Small left apical pneumothorax; small to moderate left basilar pleural effusion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: Learning Material content

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Multiple skeletal lytic lesions (including punched out lesions in left proximal humerus)
  • Diffuse osteopenia with compression fractures in the spine, and prior vertebroplasty in lumbar vertebra noted. Prior rib fractures also noted.
  • Clavicles, scapulae, ribs also demonstrate abnormalities (see marked)
  • Bibasilar linear opacification.
  • There is no airspace opacification or pleural effusion.
  • There is no evidence of a parenchymal mass or multiple pulmonary parenchymal nodules.
  • Left PICC tip in the superior vena cava.

IMPRESSION:

Multiple lytic lesions. This patient had a known history of multiple myeloma.

Linear opacities bilaterally (not the point of this case).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Multiple Myeloma is a clonal plasma cell malignancy. Typical plain film findings are:

  • "Punched-out" lesions = round/oval sharply marginated lytic defects that look like a hole "punched out" of bone. Key features: lack a sclerotic rim, no periosteal reaction, uniform lucency. See classic ones in the left proximal humerus here!
  • Endosteal cortical scalloping
  • Pathologic fractures (including vertebral compression fractures)
  • Rare sclerosis (POEMS = osteosclerotic myeloma)

Distribution of skeletal disease from most to least common: vertebrae, ribs, skull [pepper-pot calvarium], shoulders, pelvis, long bones in that order.


Dedicated MSK courses will discuss this in more detail, but the findings can also be seen on other imaging (like CXR).


CXR Findings:

  • Bony findings, including classic "punched-out" lesions / lytic lesions, pathologic fractures (ribs, spine), compression fractures, osteopenia
  • Extraskeletal manifestations more rare - extrapleural masses, effusions, apical soft tissue.


Remember to look at the bones in your checklist.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 19 - Aortic Dissection with displaced calcifications

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Aortic knuckle calcifications are separated from the outer aortic contour by > 10 mm, suspicious for aortic dissection.
  • This is also new from prior (now displaced from outer contour, previously not)
  • Although less specific, this can also be seen on the lateral in the descending aorta (compare current exam vs prior exam)

Other Findings:

  • Cardiopericardial silhouette is enlarged.
  • Lingular atelectasis. Streaky opacities in the right lower zone likely atelectasis.
  • Interval depiction of small bilateral basal pleural effusions with adjacent lung atelectasis.
  • Compression fractures, including T8 vertebral body

IMPRESSION:

Findings are consistent with aortic dissection. Suggest emergent CT to better assess.

A CT was performed that confirmed a type B dissection.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Aortic Dissection involves an intimal tear with blood entering into the media of aorta, creating a false lumen. We cover aortic dissection in the CT Chest course. CXR is very insensitive for dissection of course, and CXR is often normal in patients with dissection (CT must be performed if suspect dissection). But, there are some described signs you must be familiar with.

Calcium Sign

Most importantly, the Calcium Sign is relatively specific for dissection when seen (though low sensitivity).

It involves:

  • Intimal calcifications separated from outer aortic contour by at least 10 mm (shown in this case)
  • Comparison to prior is helpful (in this case, it was new)

Urgent CTA should be performed.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 107 - Calcified / Ossified Metastases (case of osteosarcoma metastases)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Calcified / ossified metastases, including bilateral pulmonary metastases and likely right hilar lymphadenopathy.
  • While we put arrows on a few, there are innumerable and they are very conspicuous due to high density (calcified / ossified, well defined)

Other Notes:

  • No pulmonary edema
  • No consolidation
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
  • Heart is not enlarged

This patient had a history of osteosarcoma. In that context, these are consistent with metastases.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Metastatic nodules can develop intratumoral calcium or ossification in some circumstances that are worth knowing:

  • True ossification or calcification -- including tumors with osteoid or chondroid formation, some other cancers (papillary thyroid, serous ovarian)
  • Dystrophic calcifications after necrosis or therapy
  • Mucin associated calcifications

Calcified Granulomas are common and benign. How to differentiate this case from that?

  • Benign patterns of calcification: central, diffuse calcification [most common], popcorn calcification in hamartomas (better seen at CT)
  • Favor Malignancy: Multiple nodules of variable size, calcifications may be eccentric, punctate, amorphous; interval growth; known malignancy that can calcify, post-therapy.

Clinical context matters most.

Also, CXR is going to be poor at evaluating most of these things -- CT much better of course. However in a case like this, these are obviously metastases (numerous, varying size).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 34 - Pulmonary Venous Congestion (Mild), No significant pulmonary edema

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Mild cardiac enlargement (Cardiothoracic ratio > 0.5, at 0.57), including likely left atrial enlargement.
  • No significant pulmonary edema.
  • There is mild vascular redistribution (arrows).
  • There is no airspace opacification or pleural effusion.
  • Prior right mastectomy.

IMPRESSION:

No significant pulmonary edema. Mild pulmonary venous congestion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
In CHF (Congestive Heart Failure), there is decreased cardiac output and increased pulmonary venous pressure. This results in dilated pulmonary vessels and then leakage of fluid into the lungs. This case shows only mild vascular redistribution, but no significant pulmonary edema. Know the findings at each stage.

Stage 1 (Mild) - Vascular Congestion

  • On upright films, normally upper lung veins are smaller than lower lobe veins.
  • With venous congestion, the upper veins increase in size (similar or larger), often indistinct -- referred to as "vascular redistribution". Comparison to prior is helpful. Our case only shows mild prominence.
  • On supine films, even normal patients can appear to have cephalization / redistribution, so be careful about overcalling this.
  • May also see widened vascular pedicle with fluid overload
  • Generally no edema in lungs yet

Stage 2 (Moderate) - Interstitial Edema

  • Fluid leaks into interstitial spaces --> interlobular septal thickening (Kerley B lines on Xray), peribronchial thickening

Stage 3 (Severe) - Alveolar Edema

  • Fluid eventually leaks into alveoli --> consolidation (confluent, cloud-like opacities). Distribution varies, but often perihilar.
  • Pleural effusions often also present

teachingSections.name: Cardiothoracic Ratio
teachingSections.html:
Ratio of maximum transverse diameter of heart to inner diameter of chest (just above diaphragm, widest point).

On PA films, a cardiothoracic ratio > 0.5 most commonly indicates cardiomegaly (heart too big), but can also rarely be seen in other settings (i.e. pericardial effusion).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 72 - Extensive Bronchiectasis in Cystic Fibrosis (Pre Lung Transplant)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Extensive bronchiectasis throughout, with bronchial wall thickening (ring shadows, tram-tracking)
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax
  • No airspace consolidation
  • Hyperinflated lungs (enlarged retrosternal clear space, slight diaphragm flattening)

CT correlate in same patient demonstrating these findings. Notice left upper lobe bronchiectasis with wall thickening as a correlate to our CXR (two arrows):

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is a genetic (autosomal recessive) disorder of CFTR (transporter) that results in dehydrated / viscous secretions everywhere (lung, pancreas, sinuses, etc). Lung disease is the main cause of morbidity / mortality.

Lung disease:

Poor mucociliary clearance --> recurrent infections / inflammation --> peribronchial edema, bronchiectasis, mucus impaction. Classic appearance:

  • Upper lobe predominant bilateral bronchiectasis (varicose to cystic) with bronchial thickening and mucus plugging
  • Associated areas of air trapping (on CT)

CXR Findings (Early --> Late):

  • Hyperinflation (small airway obstruction)
  • Peribronchial thickening (edema/inflammation)
  • Tram-track opacities, ring shadows = dilated and thick walled bronchi (showed in our case)
  • Mucoid impaction (branching opacities)
  • Recurrent infections
  • Advanced disease: upper lobe predominant bronchiectasis with ring shadows, cystic change, coarse reticular opacities

As mentioned, CXR is quite limited in evaluating bronchiectasis and CT is much better. But, when classic it can be noted. This case shows the classic appearance.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bilateral patchy airspace disease (consolidations), primarily perihilar in distribution (arrows) and a peribronchial distribution. Notice air bronchograms, most prominent on the left (labelled)
  • No other acute findings.

In this setting, findings are suspicious for bilateral multifocal bronchopneumonia.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We covered consolidation and air bronchograms previously.

The term "Bronchopneumonia" is a pattern of pneumonia (infection) that starts in bronchioles and involves adjacent airspace/alveoli. The classic pattern is: multifocal, bilateral, patchy, ill-defined airspace opacities with a peribronchial or perihilar predominance.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 77 - Hiatal Hernia, RLL possible pneumonia

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large hiatus hernia, with air fluid level in the left posterior chest (also see lateral)
  • Cardiomegaly, including enlarged left atrium

Other findings:

  • Aortic arch calficiations
  • Pulmonary venous congestion with prominence of pulmonary vasculature.
  • Opacification involving the lower right lung (arrows)

IMPRESSION:

Right lower lung opacification may be related to infection or aspiration. Mild pulmonary venous congestion.

Large hiatal hernia.


CT demonstrates this finding (note this CT is remote from this Xray, but shows the persistent hiatal hernia):

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Hiatal Hernia refers to herniation of abdominal contents through the esophageal hiatus and into the mediastinum -- most often the stomach herniating up. There are four types:

  • Type 1: Sliding (GE jxn slides above diaphragm) = most common by far
  • Type 2 (paraesophageal) = fundus herniates up while GE jxn stays below diaphragm
  • Type 3 (mixed sliding + paraesophageal)
  • Type 4 = large with additional organs in the chest


Appearance on CXR shown here is:

  • Retrocardiac air-fluid level with a rounded paracardiac lucency.
  • This is often left sided but often closer to the midline than shown in this case (here, enlarged heart/left atrium displaces to left more)
  • Cannot classify subtype adequately on CXR (need CT or upper GI study)

Simply report presence of suspected hiatal hernia. If uncertain, can perform CT practically.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 63 - Swan Ganz (appropriately positioned after retraction)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Chest AP Portable Findings:

  • Right-sided pulmonary artery catheter (Swan Ganz) tip is projected in the right main pulmonary artery. This is appropriately positioned, pulled back compared to prior study (see below)
  • No pneumothorax.

Other Findings:

  • Right sided PICC, tip over lower SVC
  • Small right pleural effusion with opacities in the right lower lung (was present on multiple priors)

Prior Imaging Study:

The tip of PA catheter was projecting lateral to the hilum (too deep). The catheter was retracted prior to the Xray in this case (now appropriately positioned).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced PA catheters in a prior case, including optimal tip placement in the main, proximal right, or proximal left PA. The tip is most commonly in the location shown in this case (proximal right PA).

Too proximal placement (outflow tract, RV) or too distal placement (lateral to hilum) should be adjusted.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 76 - Extrapleural chest wall mass, rib lesion

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Extrapleural soft tissue mass in the right chest wall with smooth border, forming an obtuse angle with the chest wall. While the part projecting over the lung is obvious, window and notice the abnormal soft tissue component in the lateral chest wall.
  • Associated destructive lesion of the lateral aspect of the right sixth seventh rib.
  • Tiny nodule in the periphery of the lower left lung may be calcified, likely a granuloma but indeterminate.

IMPRESSION:

Destructive lesion of the lateral aspect of the right seventh rib with extrapleural soft tissue mass.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Extrapulmonary masses typically form an obtuse angle with the chest wall/pleura (in contrast, pulmonary lesions have an acute angle). If suspected, look for signs of extrapulmonary origin, some present in our case:

  • Rib destruction or expansion
  • Pleural thickening / effusion (if pleural)
  • Displaced extrapleural fat stripe
  • Soft tissue mass visible in chest wall


Note that the smooth peripheral convexity with tapering edges is typical of extrapleural mass, shown in our case. CT should be performed to further evaluate.

cases.clinicalHistory: Post intubation and CVC (central venous catheter)
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 41 - PA catheter (Swan Ganz) slightly proximal

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Lines/Tubes:

  • Tip of ETT is 2.5 cm from the carina.
  • Right pulmonary arterial catheter (Swan Ganz) with its tip in the right ventricular outflow tract (labelled). This is a bit too proximal (ideally in PA, or proximal R/L PA). Consider repositioning.
  • Left central line (subclavian) with its tip pointing upwards into the left internal jugular vein. Consider repositioning.
  • The lower end of the NG tube is in the stomach below the diaphragm.
  • There is a midline and left basal chest drain in situ (single arrows)

Other findings:

  • Left basal consolidation (single arrow) and small left pleural effusion (double arrow). No pneumothorax.
  • Known enlarged pulmonary arteries in keeping with pulmonary hypertension.
  • Sternotomy wires noted.

OVERALL:

Pulmonary arterial catheter tip is in the right ventricular outflow tract.

Left subclavian line tip is turned upwards into the left internal jugular vein.

Consider repositioning.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pulmonary Artery (Swan Ganz) Catheter is a balloon-tipped catheter placed from a central line and into the pulmonary artery. It is used to measure hemodynamics (RA, RV, PA, wedge pressures, cardiac output) in sick patients.

Normal Course/Position:

  • From IJ/subclavian --> SVC --> RA --> Pulmonary Artery (PA)
  • Tip should be in the main, proximal right, or proximal left PA. It is most commonly in the proximal right because of the course.

Malposition:

  • Too Distal: Too peripheral if beyond the hilum, putting patient at risk for PA rupture, hemorrhage, etc
  • Too proximal: remains in RV or near PA root (can irritate valve/RV, or unreliable readings). [Our case is slightly too proximal -- we should see it turn superiorly and to the right along the course of the PA]

Follow-up study after repositioning of Swan Ganz is below, which is now a bit too lateral/distal (it should not project lateral to the hilum). There has also been interval placement of a Left IJ catheter (and removal of left subclavian line).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 101 - Peripheral and basal predominant fibrosis (Fibrotic NSIP or UIP pattern)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Interstitial reticulation (too many lines, too peripheral) throughout both lungs, but with a basal and peripheral distribution.
  • Coarse peripheral areas demonstrate coarse cystic change, suspected areas of advanced fibrosis (either traction bronchiectasis or honeycombing, much better assessed on CT).
  • **Low lung volumes / volume loss (9 posterior ribs above diaphragm)
  • There is no airspace consolidation or pleural effusion.

IMPRESSION:

Findings suggest pulmonary fibrosis, possibly with a UIP pattern. High resolution CT suggested.


CT confirmed peripheral basal predominant fibrosis with traction bronchiectasis, either fibrotic NSIP or UIP (covered in CT chest course):

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pulmonary Fibrosis involves fibrosis/ scarring in the lungs. Patterns of interstitial lung disease are beyond the scope of this course but introduced in the Chest CT course. Know how to identify fibrosis on CXR.


CXR Findings:

  • Reticular opacities are the hallmark, which progress to coarse reticulation (as shown in this case) and architectural distortion
  • Often peripheral / subpleural and basal distribution (common with UIP / NSIP patterns, but other distributions can occur)
  • Volume loss - reduced lung volumes, elevated diaphragms, crowded ribs
  • Traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing (stacked subpleural cystic change) better seen on CT

When identified, describe the distribution, degree of volume loss, and recommend HRCT (high resolution CT) for pattern classification and management planning.

teachingSections.name: Reticular Pattern (too many lines):
teachingSections.html:
• Favoring Fibrosis: volume loss, coarse reticulation, architectural distortion, peripheral / basal predominance, chronic and non-resolving, any traction bronchiectasis or honeycombing
  • Pulmonary Edema: smooth interlobular septal lines, findings associated such as cardiomegaly, effusions, consolidations if severe enough
  • Lymphangitic spread of tumor: known malignancy, nodular septal thickening
  • Atypical infection
  • Other ILDs

Clinical setting also key, of course.

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Visualized Ventriculopleural Shunt tubing is intact, with tip projecting over left pleural space.

Other Findings unchanged.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
CSF shunts are placed for long-term CSF diversion to treat hydrocephalus. Fluid drains from the ventricular system to another space. This is most commonly to peritoneal cavity (VP shunt = ventriculoperitoneal shunt, first choice) but can be non-peritoneal if needed (ventriculopleural [this case], ventriculoatrial, etc).

A "shunt series" are Xrays performed to evaluate the shunt tubing from top to bottom (often involves skull, neck, chest, and abdominal Xray). You should look for: kinks, fractures, disconnections, migration. Confirm the distal tip reaches the intended destination.

Generally, shunt series should be used selectively when there is a clinical reason to check. Routine post-op shunt series are discouraged.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Pulmonary artery enlargement centrally:

  • Marked main pulmonary artery enlargement (see bulge along left heart border at site of PA)
  • Hilar enlargement out of proportion with lung size (arrows). This is also seen on the lateral (right with single arrow, left with two arrows)

Other Findings:

  • Cardiopericardial silhouette is enlarged.
  • Descending aorta is tortuous.
  • Haziness near the right hilum is nonspecific, may represent some atypical infection. Some blunting of right CPA nonspecific, but likely from pleural thickening based on prior CT.
  • Sternotomy wires and surgical staples in the upper abdomen.

IMPRESSION:

Findings of chronic pulmonary hypertension.

No lobar consolidation. Hazy opacity in the right lung could be chronic. A superimposed atypical infection also possible.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pulmonary Hypertension refers to elevated pulmonary arterial pressure. There are many causes -- here they are (WHO groups) from most to least common:

  • Group 2 (secondary to left heart disease) - most common
  • Group 3 (secondary to lung disease)
  • Group 1 (pulmonary arterial hypertension, including idiopathic)
  • Group 4 (CTEPH = chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension; other PA obstruction)
  • Group 5 (multifactorial)

CXR is good at suggesting pulmonary hypertension but more limited for etiology unless obvious).


CXR Findings of PH:

  • Enlarged pulmonary arteries centrally (main PA, hilar)
  • Right heart enlargement - prominent right heart border
  • Peripheral vascular pruning - rapid tapering of peripheral vessels
  • PA calcifications sometimes seen when chronic

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right lower lobe airspace opacities (see subtle increased density on frontal [arrows], and on the lateral [arrows]. Remember that it should become more lucent on the lateral from superior to inferior -- here there are patchy opacities.
  • Air-fluid level depicted posterior to the right hilum (short arrows and air fluid level labelled on frontal). This is in the superior segment of the right lower lobe (see lateral with labels and short arrows) measuring at least 5 cm

Other Findings:

  • Heart is normal in size.
  • Mediastinal contours within normal limits.
  • No obvious mediastinal lymph node enlargement.
  • The left lung is clear.
  • There is no pleural effusion.
  • No evidence of bone destruction. There is a healed fracture of the right ninth posterior rib.

IMPRESSION:

Right lower lobe cavity with airspace disease surrounding it. Differential includes cavitary pulmonary infection including tuberculosis, abscess, or less likely cavitary neoplasm.

Correlation with history and further evaluation with CT suggested.


Subsequent CT demonstrates these findings nicely (below). Notice the large cavity and surrounding patchy infectious/inflammatory appearing airspace disease.

This ended up being a lung abscess with surrounding infection, caused by a contained esophageal leak.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Cavitary Lesion (pulmonary cavity) is a gas filled lucency within a nodule, mass, or area of consolidation. It often appears as a round or ovoid lucency with or without an air-fluid level (which suggests superimposed infection). CT is more sensitive, of course, and helps evaluate further.

Characteristics to report/consider:

  • Single vs Multiple (impacts ddx)
  • Location - upper lobe often in TB or cancer
  • Wall thickness - very thick walls favor malignancy, but there is overlap
  • Air-fluid level - supports superimposed infection (like our case)
  • Perilesional changes - surrounding consolidation or centrilobular nodules favors infection (like our case)
  • Associated findings (lymphadenopathy, nodules, fungus ball within, effusion, pneumothorax)

Common Causes:

  • Infectious:
  • Lung abscess, cavitary pneumonia - often have thick, shaggy walls and with air-fluid level, adjacent consolidation
  • TB - upper lobe cavitation most common with satellite nodules
  • Fungal
  • Malignancy - Lung Cancer (10-15% of squamous cell cancers cavitate)
  • Autoimmmune - classically GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis = formerly Wegener's), often multiple
  • Less common = metastases, septic emboli/infarcts, trauma (pneumatoceles).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bilateral nodular opacities, with some elongated in morphology. The left upper lobe opacity (two arrows) has a Y-shaped / tubular morphology ("finger-in-glove").

Findings may be related to bronchiectasis with mucoid impaction in this setting. In a patient with asthma, this raises the possibility of ABPA. A chest CT was recommended.


Chest CT shows multifocal bronchial dilatation with mucoid impaction, and tree-in-bud, supporting ABPA in a patient with asthma. As a pearl, note that high attenuation mucus within impacted bronchi with central bronchiectasis is highly suggestive of ABPA on CT.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Finger-in-glove (Y or V shaped opacity) refers to the appearance of a bronchocele (focally dilated bronchus) with associated mucoid impaction (filled with mucoid secretions). Branching mucus in dilated bronchi likened to 'fingers in a glove'.

This pattern can occur due to bronchial obstruction (with distal dilatation / bronchocele) or non-obstructive causes (inflammatory/infectious).

Obstructive Causes:

  • Bronchial neoplasms (hamartomas, carcinoid, lung cancer)
  • Broncholithiasis, foreign body
  • Bronchial atresia (developmental, will see finger-in-glove with associated hyperlucent lung)

Non-obstructive:

  • ABPA (Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis) - this case
  • Cystic Fibrosis


ABPA (Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis):

A hypersensitivity to aspergillus in bronchi, usually in asthma --> allergic (IgE mediated) bronchospasm.

This causes bronchiectasis, and will classically see finger-in-glove if associated mucoid impaction; often upper lobe predominant.

ABPA is often taught in the spectrum of aspergillus related diseases, though it is related to allergic response to aspergillus, so also discussed in allergic / eosinophilic lung disease spectrum.

There are diagnostic criteria for ABPA beyond the scope here (considers asthma, IgE levels, eosinophilia, etc). Management is steroids +/- antifungals. Follow-up imaging often performed to resolution.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Miliary nodules throughout both lungs (diffuse tiny nodules throughout, without zonal predilection; appearing as tiny dots throughout)
  • Heart is not enlarged.
  • No pneumothorax or pleural effusion.

Differential diagnosis includes miliary tuberculosis or fungal pneumonia in the absence of a known underlying malignancy.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case of miliary TB, which refers to hematogenous spread of TB causing inumerable micronodules throughout the lungs ('millet seeds'). It has high morbidity and mortality.

We cover TB elsewhere including in the chest CT course, but miliary TB can happen with primary or post-primary infection.

We also show this case to demonstrate the classic appearance of innumerable small nodules with a random distribution. The differential for this appearance classically includes:

  • Miliary infection (TB, fungal) d/t hematogenous spread
  • Metastases


Know the micronodular patterns in the lungs and associated Ddx (better differentiated on CT and so covered in chest CT course):

  • Centrilobular (spare subpleural / fissures)
  • Perilymphatic (predominantly concentrate on pleura, interlobular septa, bronchovascular bundles). Classic Ddx: sarcoidosis, silicosis, lymphangitic carcinomatosis
  • Random (both centrilobular and perilymphatic nodules throughout). Classic Ddx: miliary TB/fungal, metastases

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Pulmonary artery catheter (arrows) descends down a left sided SVC with tip in the pulmonary trunk (appropriate position)
  • ICD with tip in RV, also descends down left sided SVC
  • Mild cardiomegaly, with mild pulmonary vascular congestion
  • No other significant findings

We show this case to demonstrate the appearance of persistent left sided SVC on CXR.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Persistent Left Sided SVC is the most common thoracic venous anomaly (0.5% of population). It occurs due to failure of regression of an embryologic vein (left anterior cardinal vein). It descends down the left side of aortic arch and drains into the RA via dilated coronary sinus (classic trivia, 90%), but can rarely drain into LA, creating a right-to-left shunt (10%).

The appearance on CXR is classically shown with the course of an inserted line to left of aortic knob (like in this case). Normally the SVC is on the right.

Configurations:

  • Duplicated SVC (most common) - both right and left SVCs present
  • Isolated left SVC

Associations:

  • Congenital heart disease (ASD most common)
  • Conduction abnormalities
  • If drains to LA, then risk of paradoxical emboli due to shunt

Differential on CXR (line to left of aorta):

  • Line within an artery (subclavian artery) or extravascular
  • In another vein: anomalous pulmonary venous return (PAPVR) is the classic main consideration

Obviously there is no differential if do a CT and trace the vein.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Smooth round mass measuring 5 x 4 cm is seen in the right middle lobe region, with well defined margins. The horizontal fissure is tethered towards it.
  • Mild cardiomegaly.
  • Importantly, no pleural effusion or recent history of pleural effusion.
  • No other acute findings

IMPRESSION:

Right lung mass.


This was lung cancer. We show this because it mimics pleural pseudotumor ("pseudopseudotumor!?"). Remember not to overcall pleural pseudotumor -- to call it confidently on CXR it should have the classic appearance, in the right clinical situation (i.e. have evidence of pleural fluid / recent effusion or fluid overload).


CT showed an enhancing soft tissue attenuation mass with lobulated margins.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Lung masses (> 3 cm) were introduced in a previous case. We show this case because it mimics pleural pseudotumor (fluid in the fissure / fissural pseudomass). This was incorrectly called pleural fluid on the initial Xray.

We could coin the term pseudopseudotumor!?


Remember not to overcall pseudotumor! To call it, you want it to be the classic appearance (lenticular, fissural) and in the classic patient (fluid overload, recent or current effusions, etc). Either way it should be followed to resolution at bare minimum if confident. And if not, go to CT.


Here, also note that the horizontal fissure can be seen tethered towards the mass, which we see with tumors. In pleural pseudotumor, it follows the contour of the fissure and has a biconvex appearance.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate sized right subpulmonic effusion. This mimics elevated right diaphragm (see discussion in learning material). Notice lateralized peak, blunted CPA, lack of visualization of vessels behind the effusion/pseudo-diaphragm (window), tracking of fluid along undersurface of major fissure on lateral with vertical ascent anteriorly
  • Volume loss in the right middle lobe and right lower lobe, with silhouetting of the right heart border and right hemidiaphragm.

IMPRESSION:

Moderate right subpulmonic effusion with associated atelectasis in the right middle and lower lobes.


CT was performed same day for other reasons (r/o PE) and showed moderate effusion. Note CT acquired supine of course, so distribution different:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Subpulmonic (subpulmonary) effusion is a pleural effusion that accumulates between lung base and diaphragm -- layering under the lung rather than laterally. This can mimic an elevated hemidiaphragm.

Findings suggesting subpulmonic effusion over diaphragm elevation:

  • Apparent elevated diaphragm, but lateralized "peak" of dome (apex from medial to lateral third -- most accentuated on expiratory views). Lateralized peak occurs as fluid preferentially accumulates laterally.
  • Flattening of medial diaphragm contour
  • Nonvisualization of lower lobe vessels below apparent diaphragm (fluid)
  • Fluid tracking along the fissure (shown well on lateral here)

Decubitus films can help. CT and ultrasound can be performed if in doubt.

cases.clinicalHistory: Left sided lung cancer post-treatment, r/o metastases

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left perihilar opacification, and volume loss of the left hemithorax relating to known treated left lung cancer. This was unchanged from prior (not shown).
  • 1.3 cm nodule in the right upper lobe (new from prior)
  • 1.1 cm nodule overlying the lower left hemithorax (new from prior)
  • Multiple old left rib fractures.

IMPRESSION:

New pulmonary parenchymal nodules are suspicious for metastases.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: This case shows nodules in both lungs that are separate from the known primary left lung cancer. When reading these cases, you should of course compare the primary site to prior (not shown here), but you also need to look for signs of spread. Importantly, look for nodules elsewhere in the lung like demonstrated in this case (these were confirmed as metastases on CT).

cases.clinicalHistory: Short of Breath. h/o liver disease
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 108 - Lower Predominant Emphysema in Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Severe hyperlucency of the lower lungs (circles) with vascular pruning (arterial deficiency).
  • Marked hyperinflation (12 posterior ribs visible with low diaphragms that are flattened)
  • Other findings of hyperinflation seen including: increased retrosternal airspace (>2.5 cm) with increased AP dimension of the chest [barrel chested], widely spaced ribs (see frontal)

IMPRESSION:

Severe hyperlucency of the lower lungs with vascular markings limited almost entirely to the upper lungs.

The pattern is most likely panlobular emphysema, which was confirmed at CT. The patient was confirmed to have alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a classic case of lower lobe predominant emphysema in a patient with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. This gives us an opportunity to talk about emphysema and associated findings (including hyperinflation).


Emphysema refers to "permanent enlargement of airspaces with alveolar wall destruction". CT helps identify and classify 'type'. CXR is less sensitive but will see associated features and more advanced disease:

  • Hyperinflation (features shown in this case, including flattened hemidiaphragms, increased retrosternal airspace, etc)
  • Parenchymal / Vascular changes: hyperlucent lungs (including bullae when present), vascular pruning / arterial deficiency.

Patterns of Emphysema (On CT [see CT Chest Course]):

  • Centrilobular - most common (focal low attenuation in centre of pulmonary lobule), classically smoking-related, often upper lobe predominant
  • Panlobular - uniform involvement throughout lobule; classically associated with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, often lower lobe predominant (this case)
  • Paraseptal - subpleural / juxtaseptal low attenuation with or without bullae; often coexists with centrilobular.

Remember this is a CT diagnosis based on lobule anatomy. CXR can only suggest based on distribution, mostly in severe cases such as this.


Alpha-1 Antitrypsin deficiency is a genetic condition. Alpha-1 antitrypsin normally blocks proteases (like neutrophil elastase) that digest structural proteins (like elastin in the lung). So... a deficiency results in alveolar wall destruction. Build up in the liver also results in liver injury (and eventually cirrhosis).

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Medical Devices:

  • Midline sternotomy wires intact. Prosthetic Mitral Valve.
  • Intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) in-situ with the distal tip at the aortic knob. This may be slightly high (ideal position = just distal to knob, about 1-2 cm above the carina and 2-3 cm distal to subclavian angle -- this one nearly abuts subclavian angle).
  • There is a right transjugular pulmonary arterial catheter (Swan Ganz) in-situ with the distal tip in the main pulmonary artery.
  • Endotracheal tube in-situ with the distal tip 7 cm above the carina.
  • Mediastinal drains in place.
  • Temporary epicardial pacing wires are noted.

IMPRESSION:

Intra-aortic balloon pump with tip at the aortic knuckle. The tip may be very slightly too high - consider withdrawing very slightly to avoid occlusion of the left subclavian artery.


This was adjusted slightly. See ideal positioning in follow-up below:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP) is a catheter with a balloon that sits in the descending thoracic aorta. It inflates in diastole to boost coronary / organ perfusion, and deflates in systole to reduce resistance/afterload. They are inserted via femoral artery.

Tip Positioning:

  • Tip (radiopaque marker) should be in proximal descending aorta about 2-3 cm distal to subclavian angle (just distal to aortic knob; usually 1-2 cm above carina).
  • Distally (if included) should sit above renal arteries
  • Too high (at or above arch/knob) = risk compromise of head/neck vessels

Appearance:

  • Balloon is radiolucent, but will see radiopaque markers at proximal (and distal) end.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Lines/Tubes:

  • NG tube tip curled retrograde and lies within the distal esophagus (see arrows/labels). The tube should be repositioned.
  • Right IJ approach central venous catheter with tip projected over the SVC.

Other findings:

  • Mild bibasilar atelectasis.
  • Trace bilateral pleural effusions
  • Prominent gaseous bowel loops in the partially visualized upper abdomen.
  • Midline surgical skin clips noted in the lower chest/upper abdomen.

IMPRESSION:

NG tube tip curled retrograde and within distal esophagus. The tube should be repositioned.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates a malpositioned NG tube. We cover NG tubes in the Abdominal Xray course as well, but this will of course also be seen on chest Xray.

Optimal position of an NG tube will be with tip and side-hole within the stomach. The tip should be about 10 cm beyond the GE junction.

In this case, the tube can be followed into the stomach, after which it curls retrograde and tip sits in the esophagus, with side-hole labelled. This requires repositioning.

NG Malposition:

  • Airway - most commonly right bronchial tree.
  • Esophagus (too short), requires advancement
  • Coiling in pharynx
  • Post-pyloric when gastric intended (sometimes this is intentional if need postpyloric placement).

The right IJ central catheter tip should ideally be in the lower SVC / near cavoatrial junction. These are less frequently in tributaries like azygos than PICCs (because PICCs travel all the way from the arm).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Multiple bilateral well defined round nodules (cannonball pattern) in both lungs, consistent with metastases
  • Notice the varying conspicuity of metastases, and how windowing makes retrocardiac and retrodiaphragmatic metastases much more conspicuous.

IMPRESSION:

Multiple bilateral lung metastases.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case shows multiple bilateral round circumscribed lung nodules - referred to as "cannonball" metastases. The pattern reflects hematogenous spread, and is most classically associated with certain primaries. Most classic are RCC and choriocarcinoma; but also colorectal, breast, endometrial, prostate, sarcomas.

In the real world, it is more important to just call these metastases and then search for a primary (i.e. the 'cannonball' feature is really not super clinically important IMO - but it has been described and so worth noting).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left PICC (peripherally inserted central catheter) with tip projecting over the azygos arch (zoom in). Notice the catheter turns superiorly, medially, and posteriorly (latter would be better seen on lateral) into expected site of azygos arch. Remember from CT chest anatomy that the azygos courses just above the right mainstem bronchus. This should be repositioned.

Other Findings:

  • Right inferolateral pleural effusion with blunting of the right costophrenic angle (in this case, unchanged from prior). Associated linear atelectasis.
  • No pneumothorax

IMPRESSION:

Left PICC tip is in the azygos arch. Consider repositioning.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A PICC (Peripherally Inserted Central Catheter) is a venous catheter placed for long term IV therapy (chemo, antibiotics, etc). Xrays are commonly performed to confirm position, and you will see these all the time on studies done for other reasons.

Normal Tip Position:

  • Ideally, tip should be at cavoatrial junction (lower third of SVC, often about 3 cm below carina)

Malposition:

  • Right Atrium (too low) - increased risk of arrhythmias, perf, malfunction. Ideally withdrawn to cavoatrial junction
  • Internal Jugular - catheter extends cranially into neck
  • Azygos Vein (this case) - from SVC, curves posteriorly/medially just above right mainstem. This is a smaller calibre vein and should be repositioned
  • Contralateral brachiocephalic vein (crosses midline)

Note that it doesn't need to be "exact" and there is some interobserver variability here. But, suggest repositioning if above the mid to upper SVC or clearly within the RA. See this image.

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large right pneumothorax (arrows)
  • Moderate right pleural effusion.
  • Marked leftward shift of the mediastinum, consistent with tension pneumothorax notwithstanding the presence of a large pleural effusion (i.e. the marked amount of shift is out of proportion with what would be expected from that moderate effusion alone).
  • Dual lead pacemaker present

IMPRESSION:

Large right tension pneumothorax with contralateral mediastinal shift.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Tension Pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency. Large pneumothorax --> progressive lung collapse --> compression of mediastinal structures --> impairs venous return and cardiac output (hence life threatening without decompression). It is a clinical diagnosis - imaging should not delay management (urgent decompression), but you will see it on imaging and will see findings of increased intrapleural pressure.

CXR Findings:

  • Large ipsilateral pneumothorax with near complete lung collapse
  • Contralateral mediastinal shift indicates tension
  • Other signs of tension: flattening of ipsilateral diaphragm, sharp mediastinal border, splaying of ribs (see greater distance btw ribs in our case)

Classic cases don't often have associated pleural effusion, which makes our case a bit tougher. Here's a classic example of Tension Pneumo without an effusion.


NOTE: Pneumothorax + pleural effusion together on the same side is often referred to as "hydropneumothorax".

cases.clinicalHistory: Lung cancer f/u, prior right upper lobectomy

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Persistent opacification and volume loss of the upper remaining right lung
  • Area of increasing opacification overlying the medial lower right lung on the frontal examination. [For context, this was increasing compared to prior studies, priors not shown here]
  • Increased hazy opacification with interstitial reticulation of the mid and lower left lung suspicious for lymphangitic carcinomatosis.

Other Findings:

  • No pulmonary edema
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Increasing opacification of the medial right lung base. Increasing reticulation in mid to lower left lung, suggesting lymphangitic carcinomatosis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Lymphangitic carcinomatosis refers to tumor spread (usually adenocarcinoma including lung, breast, GI, etc) through lung lymphatics. This appears as nodular/irregular septal thickening on CT (see CT chest course). CXR is much less sensitive to this (Xray often normal early on), but you may see increased reticular / nodular pattern on serial Xrays.

In our case, there were progressive opacities including in the right lower lung and interstitial thickening in the left lung -- all consistent with worsening malignancy. We show it to demonstrate the appearance of a confirmed case to demonstrate what you might be looking for.

Of course, the clinical context and comparison with priors is key for these cases, especially for differentiating post treatment change (stable or retract) vs tumor (increase over time).

CT would help better assess if suspicious.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 80 - Thyroid Goitre, Positive Cervicothoracic Sign

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Thickening of right paratracheal stripe with a right paratracheal mass in the superior mediastinum that results in mass effect on trachea, with leftward deviation.
  • The mass appears contiguous with lower neck, and there are calcifications noted within the mass (window hard)
  • Above the clavicles, the mass has indistinct / fuzzy borders (positive cervicothoracic sign)

Other Findings:

  • Left ventricular enlargement
  • Aortic arch calcification
  • No pulmonary edema, consolidation.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Superior mediastinal mass is likely a thyroid goitre. CT can be performed to confirm and better assess given extension into mediastinum.


CT was performed and demonstrated a heterogeneously enlarged thyroid extending into the mediastinum. The key is that the mass is contiguous with the thyroid:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates an anterior/superior mediastinal mass that was a thyroid goitre extending into the mediastinum. Enlarged thyroid can extend down into superior mediastinum / down to anterior mediastinum.

The key is that it is contiguous with the neck, and on CT demonstrates direct contiguity.

Cervicothoracic Sign:

"Cervicothoracic Sign" helps determine if a superior mediastinal mass is anterior or posterior to trachea. It can be confusing but is an important principle:

  • Since the anterior mediastinum ends at clavicles, an anterior mass that extends above the clavicles into the neck will have indistinct borders (positive cervicothoracic sign = borders 'disappear' as extend above clavicles)
  • If well delineated borders above clavicles = posterior mediastinal (outlined by lung above clavicles means it has to be posterior, because posterior mediastinum ends higher posteriorly)
  • If that doesn't make sense, look at the lateral projection and see how the posterior mediastinal lung extends higher

In this case, notice indistinct margins above clavicles, indicating anterior.


Goitres typically occupy superior/anterior mediastinum, so show fuzzy border above clavicles, demonstrate paratracheal thickening, may obliterate anterior clear space if extend inferior enough, and deviate the trachea. CT confirms.

cases.clinicalHistory: NG tube insertion, known right chest wall mass
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 70 - NG tube malposition, deep sulcus sign

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
On this supine portable Xray:

  • NG tube is malpositioned, extending into left lower airways through left mainstem bronchus (arrows)
  • Deep sulcus sign on the left - lateral costophrenic angle is abnormally deep and lucent. This indicates pneumothorax on supine film, due to gas tracking anteriorly into anterolateral subpulmonic recess.
  • Note that multiple radiographs were taken during NG placement. On earlier radiograph ('pre line adjustment'), there was no pneumothorax. The malpositioned NG was advanced, which created the pneumothorax.
  • There is also a large right chest wall mass

An upright Xray confirmed a moderate pneumothorax shortly after on the left (after NG tube removal):

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a nice case showing two important things: NG malposition (also covered in abdominal Xray course) and the classic deep sulcus sign of pneumothorax on supine film.


Deep Sulcus Sign:

Recall that gas rises / moves anti-dependent. When a patient is supine, pleural air will not move to the apex (like we see with upright films). Instead, it moves anteriorly, and accentuates the lateral costophrenic recess (hence "deep" sulcus with lower attenuation).


The sensitivity of supine films for pneumothorax is of course much lower than upright. But, you need to know the classic sign (as shown in this case).

Importantly, the 'depth' alone can be mimicked by patient position. To call this, you would want to see a deeper, sharper CPA on supine films that is ALSO very lucent. When in doubt, get an upright film like was done in this case.


NG Tube Malposition:

We cover this in the abdominal Xray course, but an NG tube in airway (usually through right mainstem, but can be on the left like in our case) is urgent and needs to be communicated to the team. The tube must not be used (can cause aspiration pneumonia-like lung disease) and risk for pneumothorax. In this case, it was not noted at initial insertion at the bedside and was advanced, causing pneumothorax.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 51 - LV aneurysm, Right sided Pneumonia

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left sided ICD (notice two shock coils) with single lead tip in the right ventricle
  • Cardiomegaly
  • Abnormal contour bulge at left heart border (left ventricle) with wide-necked outpouching and peripheral calcification (see lateral)
  • Hazy airspace opacities in the right middle and lower zone projecting over the right middle and lower lobe
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Right middle and lower lung airspace opacities are suspicious for pneumonia.

Cardiomegaly with calcified LV aneurysm.

Note that the LV aneurysm was seen at a same day CT:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
LV Aneurysm refers to bulging of a thinned, scarred LV wall after MI. Wall consists of scarred myocardium and endocardium (hence 'true' aneurysm).

  • It typically has a wide neck, is akinetic/dyskinetic
  • It may calcify or contain thrombus.
  • Most common location is anterior / apical (LAD territory)


LV Pseudoaneurysm is a contained free-wall rupture. The sac is composed of pericardium / adhesions (hence 'pseudo'aneurysm) rather than myocardium/endocardium.

  • More often has a narrow neck
  • Higher risk of rupture, so requires more urgent repair
  • Predilection for inferior, posterior, lateral walls (less often apical)


Both are nonspecific at CXR. Often see cardiomegaly with abnormal left heart border contour or bulge. Calcification along aneurysm may suggest chronic true aneurysm. Differentiation requires CT, MRI, or echo, but generally: wide neck (true) vs narrow neck (pseudo).


Basic Terminology in General: Aneurysm vs Pseudoaneurysm

  • Aneurysm - focal dilatation with outpouching containing normal histologic vessel layers
  • Pseudoaneurysm - contained rupture where blood exits and does not contain all layers (usually thrombus, periarterial tissues), hence 'false' aneurysm

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• There is a fracture through the upper lateral margin of the left scapula with no significant fragment displacement.

Other Findings:

  • Heart is not enlarged.
  • No consolidation
  • No pneumothorax or pleural effusion

IMPRESSION:

Left scapular fracture

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: Remember to look at all of the bones in your search pattern. This case demonstrates a left scapular fracture post-trauma.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left chest tube present, with interval drainage of left pleural effusion (prior not shown), with no significant residual effusion.
  • Hazy airspace opacification of the left lung with basilar opacification likely airspace consolidation and atelectasis.
  • No evidence of a pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Mild re-expansion pulmonary edema of the left lung.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Re-expansion pulmonary edema occurs when collapsed lung rapidly re-expands (from atelectasis/effusion, pneumothorax), leading to pulmonary edema.

On CXR, it will appear as new unilateral interstitial and alveolar opacities in the re-expanded lung -- shortly after re-expansion.

In this case, we see mild airspace opacities at site of re-expanded lung.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tiny bilateral pleural effusions, best seen as blunting of costophrenic angles on lateral projection.

Other Findings:

  • Heart is not enlarged.
  • History revealed recent esophagectomy with gastric pullup. Note mild dilatation of the esophagus / gastric pullup (see lateral behind trachea, and frontal)
  • No focal parenchymal opacities.
  • No evidence of a pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum.

IMPRESSION:

Tiny bilateral pleural effusions.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pleural effusions refer to fluid in the pleural space. Fluid layers dependently and is free-flowing. On upright films, that results in fluid blunting costophrenic angles (CPA), meniscus sign, silhouetting of diaphragm, mediastinal shift away.

A few things to know:

  • **Lateral is more sensitive than PA - will see blunting of CPA with about 50-75 mL of fluid on lateral (vs > about 200 mL if CPA blunting on frontal).
  • Supine films less sensitive. Fluid layers posteriorly, resulting in more diffuse basal hazy density (supine only).
  • Can be simple/free flowing (generally dependent, meniscus sign) or complex / loculated (consider if non-dependent tracking, lentiform/ovoid).
  • Lateral decubitus even more sensitive than lateral, can confirm 'free flowing'.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 3 - Luftsichel Sign (Left Upper Lobe Collapse)

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

data.text: Increased attenuation due to LUL collapse

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Complete collapse of the left upper lobe (Luftsichel sign). This is described in greater detail in the learning material.
  • Also notice other findings of volume loss (elevation of left hemidiaphragm, mediastinal shift to the left)

Other findings:

  • Nodular opacities noted in the right mid and upper lung fields.
  • No pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Notice that the right scapular border mimics a pneumothorax / pleural line (short arrows). Also, zoom in to see that there are clearly lung markings beyond that.

Lung cancer was confirmed at CT and subsequent biopsies.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Luftsichel sign is a classic sign of complete left upper lobe collapse that you must know (for real life and exams). Luftsichel means "air crescent" in German, referring to lucency between the aortic arch and increased opacity of the lung collapse. Understand its appearance:

  • Left upper lobe collapses in an anterior/superior direction, resulting in increased attenuation of left lung
  • Compensatory hyperinflation of superior segment of left lower lobe, which surrounds the aortic arch/knucke -- the so called 'air crescent' of luftsichel sign

To understand the appearance you need to know the anatomy. The images from this paper show it nicely. Here is figure 3 from this open-access paper:

This lateral image also shows you why the entire left lung looks increased in attenuation.

What does it mean?

Complete LUL collapse should prompt looking for a cause.

Most classically, this is an obstructing mass / neoplasm (usually cancer).

However, just like lobar collapse elsewhere there are other causes (i.e. if acute and new from a recent prior, it could be a mucus plug or infectious -- but must be followed to ensure resolution!).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large mediastinal soft tissue mass (silhouetting mediastinal structures, obtuse angles with mediastinum) sits in anterior mediastinum (loss of retrosternal clear space on lateral, sitting anterior to trachea which is pushed posteriorly)
  • Contains foci of calcification including more confluent calcification centrally and rim calcification (see foci of calcification on frontal)

Other Findings:

  • Lung parenchyma is clear
  • No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Large anterior mediastinal mass with calcifications.


CT demonstrated a large mass with foci of fat and both rim-like calcification and more confluent calcification (including tooth-like, see coronal inferior calcification). This is most consistent with Teratoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a large anterior mediastinal mass. The first step is to identify it as mediastinal and localize the compartment, as discussed in a prior case.

Recall the Classic Ddx:

  • Thymus related
  • Thyroid related
  • Teratoma (& other germ cell tumors)
  • Terrible Lymphoma

Calcifications themselves are not specific for an etiology. Thymic tumors can have coarse calcifications, Teratomas often have calcifications or even teeth, thyroid lesions can have calcifications, and uncommon to have calcifications in untreated lymphoma.


This patient had CT which demonstrated a large mass containing fat and calcifications, consistent with Teratoma.


Teratoma & Germ Cell Tumors:

Extragonadal Germ Cell Tumors often arise in the anterior mediastinum of young adults. You can have seminoma, non-seminomatous types (yolk sac, embryonal, choriocarcinoma, etc).

Classic Appearance:

  • Teratoma - fat and calcification on CT, often b9
  • Seminoma - bulky homogeneous soft tissue mass
  • Non-seminomatous - heterogeneous, necrotic often, check tumor markers (AFP, beta HCG) to support diagnosis


Fat containing anterior mediastinal masses:

  • Teratoma is the classic one (example in this case), may show fat-fluid level
  • Thymolipoma - large encapsulated mass that is predominantly fat and 'drapes' around the heart
  • Others more rare (lipoma is all bulk fat, liposarcoma rare)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left lung mass measures up to 4.8 cm, perihilar on frontal and in superior segment of lower lobe based on lateral.
  • Some spiculated margins including along inferior and superior mass (see lines radiating from mass)
  • No obvious mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.
  • VP shunt catheter visible

IMPRESSION:

Left lung spiculated mass, most consistent with primary lung malignancy. Suggest CT for further characterization and staging.

CT confirmed spiculated mass, a primary lung cancer:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Lung Mass is a focal rounded opacity measuring > 3 cm (VS nodule rounded opacity 3 cm or less).

On CXR:

  1. Verify true mass and locate (identify on two views if possible, like on this study)
  2. Morphology - size, number (solitary vs multiple), shape.
  3. Margins: smooth, lobulated, spiculated
  4. Density features: any calcifications, cavitation, air bronchograms, associated atelectasis
  5. Associated Findings: Adenopathy, volume loss, pleural effusion, pneumothorax
  6. Compare to prior

Margin Terminology:

  • Spiculated = irregular 'spikes' or lines radiating from nodule to adjacent lung (desmoplastic reaction / fibrosis) - very strongly associated with malignancy
  • Lobulated = multiple convex bulges along edges, reflecting uneven growth rates. Increases malignancy risk but can also be seen in b9 entities like hamartoma.

Ddx:

  • Malignancy (primary cancer, often spiculated upper lobe mass; metastasis; lymphoma)
  • Benign neoplasm (hamartoma, pseudotumor)
  • Infectious/inflammatory mass (round pneumonia, TB/fungal can be masslike w cavitation, sarcoid conglomerate masses)
  • Vascular / other mimics (AVM, infarct, round atelectasis, etc)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Asymmetric density in the left lung apex
  • Associated adjacent bony destruction (cervical spine and 1st rib)
  • Mass is also well seen on lateral Xray

Other Findings:

  • 1 cm nodule left upper lung, was stable on multiple priors
  • No other significant findings

IMPRESSION:

Findings highly suspicious for a left Pancoast tumour with rib destruction. A CT is recommended to further assess.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Pancoast Tumor ('superior sulcus tumor' or 'pulmonary apex tumor') refers to a lung cancer arising in the apex that invades adjacent chest wall (often involving first/second ribs, vertebral bodies, subclavian vessels, brachial plexus). Most are NSCLC (non-small cell lung cancers).

Clinical:

These can present with "Pancoast Syndrome" (about 25%): shoulder/arm pain, weakness of hand muscles, ipsilateral horner syndrome (ptosos, miosis, anhidrosis) [due to brachial plexus and sympathetic chain involvement].

Treatment typically neoadjuvant chemo and en bloc surgical resection when resectable.

Imaging:

  • Apical mass / opacity or "pleural cap" +/-
  • Destruction of 1st/2nd ribs or adjacent vertebral bodies
  • CT to confirm mass and bony involvement. MRI to better assess brachial plexus

Note that our case nicely shows these typical findings.


Important: Apical Scarring vs Pancoast Tumor

You will see apical scarring not infrequently, whereas pancoast tumors are not that common but essential to identify. Differentiating the two is key:

  • Benign apical cap / scarring = thin, smooth, triangular opacity; often bilateral and increases with age, may have volume loss / fibrosis
  • Pancoast tumor: asymmetric, bulky opacity/mass (see lateral here), pleural thickening > 2 cm or > 2 cm thicker than other side, bony destruction, clinical presentation different.

Here on both frontal and lateral, the apical opacity has convex margins and is > 2 cm, with frank bony destruction. There is no differential here -- i.e. this is highly suspicious for malignancy and likely a pancoast tumor (confirmed thereafter).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• No consolidation.
  • Very dense opacities noted in bilateral mid/lower lungs, branching morphology, likely related to prior barium aspiration.

Other Findings:

  • No other acute findings
  • Stable cardiopericardial silhouette and mediastinal contour.
  • No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

No radiographic evidence of pneumonia.

Evidence of prior barium aspiration in both lungs.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
During a barium swallow, aspirated contrast can enter the airway/lungs and deposit. The result is very dense (radiopaque) opacities in the dependent lung / tracheobronchial tree. Barium is not absorbed, so it often remains present for long periods.


At time of aspiration, patients are generally asymptomatic with small volumes. But patients can be symptomatic (fever, coughing, hypoxia, even ARDS can be precipitated rarely) with higher volumes.


Practically, there isn't much of a differential here. This appearance is consistent with prior aspirated barium.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 23 - Migratory Consolidations [Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia in this case]

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms (see lateral for localization), with some middle lobe involvement (see obscuration of right heart border)
  • On prior study, there was left lower lobe consolidation (retrocardiac), also seen on lateral and prior CT (see below). This has resolved on current study (compare to current)


IMPRESSION:

Interval resolution of previous left lower lobe consolidation, with interval development of right lower lobe consolidation. Considerations include recurrent pneumonia, but organizing pneumonia also possible.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case of migratory consolidations. We introduced this in the last case.


Recall that Migratory Consolidations refer to those that change locations / lobes between exams. There is some overlap in the ddx, with primary considerations of COP and CEP:

  • Organizing Pneumonia - hallmark of migratory consolidations with peribronchial and subpleural distribution
  • Eosinophilic Pneumonias - specifically Loffler syndrome (simple pulmonary eosinophilia) produces peripheral migratory consolidations. A deep dive into eosinophilic lung diseases is beyond the scope here.
  • Atypical or recurrent infections
  • Vasculitis


We cover COP / organizing pneumonia more generally in the CT Chest course.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Subtle hazy opacities in perihilar and mid to lower lungs bilaterally

In the setting of known HIV, findings raise suspicion for PJP.

CT was performed that better demonstrates the GGO:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) is an opportunistic fungal pneumonia in immunocompromised patients (classically HIV with low CD4 [an AIDS defining illness], but also transplant, steroids/biologics).

Xrays are often normal or subtly abnormal early, lower sensitivity. Classic CT findings are bilateral perihilar ground glass +/- thin walled cysts (see CT Chest course).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Ventriculoatrial shunt in the right neck with tip in the region of the tricuspid valve.
  • Partially imaged ventriculoperitoneal shunt in the lateral right neck and overlying the right chest.
  • Both catheters appear intact.

Other Findings:

  • Surgical clips in the right breast.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: We cover CSF shunts in the prior case and in the abdominal Xray course (VP shunts). We show this case to demonstrate the appearance of a ventriculoatrial shunt, though uncommon.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 17 - Sickle Cell with Acute Chest Syndrome

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Airspace opacities in right mid to lower lung zone (also see on lateral).
  • No pneumothorax.
  • H-shaped vertebral bodies (see frontal and lateral) consistent with history of sickle cell disease.

Findings are consistent with acute chest syndrome in the setting of sickle cell disease.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Acute Chest Syndrome is an acute lung injury in Sickle Cell disease. It appears as a new infiltrate on Chest Xray in the setting of fever / respiratory symptoms. A variety of things can 'precipitate' this: infections (bacterial, viral), pulmonary infarct, etc; but just refer to it as a new consolidation in keeping with acute chest syndrome in the setting of sickle cell disease.

Imaging Appearance:

  • New airspace opacity / true consolidation, often unilateral but can be bilateral. Lower lung predominance in adults.
  • Background findings from sickle cell, including:
  • H shaped vertebral bodies (shown nicely in this case) - due to central endplate infarction / necrosis from microvascular occlusion (central depression of both endplates = biconcave H shaped appearance on the lateral)
  • Other bony findings (due to bone infarcts)
  • Cardiomegaly (elevated cardiac output)


Recall that Sickle Cell disease involves abnormal hemoglobin --> RBCs 'sickle' under stress (hypoxia, infection) and cause slow flow and small vessel occlusion (microvascular).
