So, in the chest you can get microvascular occlusion triggered by an insult (infection for example). H shaped vertebrae are due to microinfarcts of central vertebral endplates. Painful bony infarcts are self explanatory.

cases.clinicalHistory: Follow-up known bilateral lower lobe opacities, RUL consolidation/atelectasis
cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 93 - Continuous Diaphragm Sign (Pneumomediastinum or Pneumopericardium)

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Air along the diaphragm crossing the midline (continuous diaphragm sign - arrows).

Other Findings:

  • Complete opacification of the right upper lobe with some volume loss, likely some atelectasis (known from prior)
  • Moderate ill defined opacities in the mid to lower lungs bilaterally (known from prior)
  • ETT, central line, weighted tip NG tube present and in place.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Continuous diaphragm sign refers to uninterrupted visualization of the diaphragm under the heart. This is classically due to pneumomediastinum or pneumopericardium. But a variant is also described with pneumoperitoneum (gas on undersurface of diaphragm).

We cover pneumomediastinum and pneumopericardium elsewhere. We include this case just to show this sign.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 1 - Right mainstem bronchus intubation, left lung collapse

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tip of the endotracheal tube (ETT) is seen within the right mainstem bronchus (also seen better here)
  • Opacification throughout the left lung, representing atelectasis / collapse of the left lung. Mediastinum is shifted to the left.
  • No pneumothorax

Findings are in keeping with right mainstem bronchus intubation. The tube was removed -- see the follow-up Xray from 2 days later in the learning material.


Aside: see this image with post-processing performed to enhance visualization of lines and tubes. Essentially, it boosts anything with a sharp edge (like lines and tubes) by amplifying high frequency bands, and suppresses low frequency bands (why the heart looks 'gray'). Use this image for lines and tubes. Don't use this image to diagnose pneumonia. You will see these on several of our case examples.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Right mainstem bronchus intubation occurs when the endotracheal tube (ETT) is advanced past the carina and into the right mainstem bronchus. As a consequence, the left lung collapses (not ventilated), and the right can become overinflated.

Findings:

  • ETT below the carina (ideal position = 3-5 cm above carina with neutral head position)
  • Unilateral whiteout (left), like in our case. Notice mediastinal shift to the left as well due to collapse / volume loss
  • Management is to withdraw the ETT and image to confirm position

Increase your understanding:

Why Right?

  • The right mainstem bronchus is more vertical and shorter (and wider). It's the same reason why aspiration preferentially impacts R > L

Normal position:

  • Aim for 3-5 cm above the carina
  • This avoids too low / bronchial intubation, or accidental extubating or vocal cord injury if too high)
  • Generally 2-7 cm okay.

Head position?

  • Neck flexion --> tip moves down (roughly 1 cm)
  • Neck extension --> tip moves up (roughly 1 cm)
  • In practical radiology, you may not know head position (in this case you can see its flexed!), so remembering a target of 3-5 cm is reasonable. You basically want to protect against tips too close to carina, or too high.

Follow-up Xray (Normal):

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right basal thick walled pleural collection with air-fluid level (arrows) is suspicious for a right-sided empyema (favored over lung abscess). Also see on frontal.
  • Right basal atelectasis/consolidation.
  • Left lung is clear.
  • The cardiopericardial silhouette is not enlarged.

IMPRESSION:

Right-sided empyema with underlying atelectasis/consolidation. This may be better evaluated with chest CT.

CT confirmed empyema:

This was successfully drained with chest tube:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Empyema is a collection of pus in the pleural space.

With pneumonia, you can have adjacent pleural collection that can be:

  • Simple parapneumonic effusion (uninfected free flowing fluid)
  • Complicated parapneumonic effusion (infected but non-purulent)
  • Empyema (infected and purulent)

Imaging can help differentiate complicated or empyema from simple with pleural enhancement on CT (split pleura sign), loculation, lenticular shape.


In our case, the pleural collection is thick walled, not free flowing (loculated posteriorly), and has an air-fluid level (gas-forming organism). Air-fluid levels are uncommon but specific when seen. CT showed pleural enhancement and air-fluid level as well.


Lung Abscess vs Empyema:

Empyema: Lentiform, smooth walls, obtuse angle with chest wall, compresses lung, split pleura sign on CT

Lung Abscess: round/oval within lung parenchyma, acute angle with chest wall, often thick walled.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Well circumscribed, dense, calcified, oval shaped nodule in the right upper lung measuring 9 mm.

Other Findings:

  • No pleural effusion.
  • The cardiopericardial contour and mediastinal contour are unremarkable.
  • No focal consolidation.

IMPRESSION:

Solitary pulmonary nodule in right upper lung, densely calcified, likely a granuloma. If this is a new finding and clinical indicated, consider CT for further characterization.


This was a calcified granuloma (benign).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Solitary Pulmonary Nodule (SPN) is a single intrapulmonary opacity measuring 3 cm or less surrounded by lung. If > 3 cm, it is called a "mass".

The primary role of CXR is to detect the nodule. CT is much more sensitive for characterization (i.e. presence and pattern of calcification, margins, size, etc). So, practically, if detect an SPN then compare to priors and if not previously characterized, then do a CT.


Benign features:

  • Dense central or complete calcification (granuloma)
  • Popcorn calcifications
  • Fat (on CT) in hamartoma
  • Long term stability

Malignant features:

  • Spiculated margins or lobulated
  • New, growing, large (i.e. mass)
  • Hilar or other adenopathy
  • Lack of b9 features


Differential: SPN

  • Malignancy (primary lung cancer, solitary metastasis)
  • Benign neoplasm (e.g. hamartoma, often fat-containing on CT)
  • Calcified Granuloma, other infectious etiologies
  • Inflammatory: GPA/Wegener's, Rheumatoid nodule, sarcoid

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Focal consolidation in the left perihilar lung, consistent with pneumonia.
  • Bilateral lung nodules, including a 2 cm retrodiaphragmatic nodule on the right side (narrow window first, then see lateral as well), consistent with lung metastasis. There are other metastases as well (arrows)

Other Findings:

  • Right Port-A-Cath with tip projected in the cavoatrial junction.
  • Normal cardiomediastinal contour.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Focal consolidation left perihilar, suggestive of pneumonia. Lung metastases.

CT was performed that shows the consolidation and nodules. There are many nodules seen on CT that are difficult to make out on CXR:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates a sizable nodule in another common blind spot: retrodiaphragmatic. This is another review area for every case that may require windowing to see better. The case also demonstrates pneumonia.

Notice the low sensitivity of CXR for nodules compared to CT!

Aside: Port-A-Cath - an implantable venous access device with a subQ reservoir connected to a central venous catheter. This allows repeated access for chemotherapy and long term IV therapy or blood draws without an external line. Tip should be near cavoatrial junction.

Common complications to look for are malposition, kinking/fracture of tubing, pneumothorax post insertion.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Marked pre and post stenotic dilatation of the aorta related to coarctation (stenosis). This gives a "Figure 3 Sign" on frontal radiograph (labelled).
  • You can actually see this on the lateral as well
  • No evidence for significant rib notching.
  • No cardiac enlargement.
  • No pulmonary venous congestion.
  • No airspace consolidation or pleural effusion.

IMPRESSION:

Aortic coarctation. No rib notching.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Aortic Coarctation is congenital narrowing of the aorta, usually at the isthmus / just distal to subclavian artery. The result is upper body hypertension (proximal to stenosis) and reduced distal perfusion (distal to stenosis). It is associated with bicuspid aortic valve.

Chest Xray Findings:

  • Aorta: Pre-Stenotic dilatation, Narrowing at Stenosis, and Post-Stenotic Dilatation. This results in "Figure 3 sign" at left mediastinal contour
  • Bilateral Rib Notching (posterior, sparing ribs 1-2) only with established collaterals (often older children / adults) - not seen on this case
  • Why Notching? Stenosis results in arterial collaterals over time (to perfuse distal aorta / lower body) -- including subclavian --> internal thoracic arteries --> flow retrograde via intercostal arteries to aorta.
  • Enlarged intercostal arteries put pulsatile pressure on rib --> pressure erosion / rib notching.
  • Possible secondary signs: cardiomegaly, LV hypertrophy


What else to know?

  • CXR can suggest, but echo, CT, MRI better to define anatomy, gradients, collaterals.
  • Untreated disease has poor prognosis
  • Treatment options include surgery (resect with end-to-end anastomosis), stenting.
  • Even after repair, patients can present with hypertension, re-coarctation, aneurysm formation (need lifelong surveillance).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Medical Devices:

  • Clamshell sternotomy wires and sternotomy fixation plate in-situ.
  • Mediastinal and surgical skin clips noted
  • ETT tip 2.2 cm proximal to carina
  • Left subclavian central venous catheter tip projects in left innominate vein
  • Left cervical approach PA catheter tip projects over proximal right main pulmonary artery
  • Nasogastric tube in-situ, tip in stomach.
  • There are 3 wide bore right-sided chest tubes and 2 wide bore left-sided chest tubes.

Other Findings:

  • Status post double lung transplant.
  • Pulmonary vascular congestion with diffuse hazy airspace opacities in both lungs, likely a combination of pulmonary edema and atelectasis. This is expected early post lung transplant, representing reperfusion injury / non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
  • Small bilateral pneumothoraces likely present with deep sulci on this supine view, left > right. Small pneumothoraces are also expected.
  • Small volume subcutaneous emphysema in the right chest wall, trace volume subcutaneous emphysema on the left.

IMPRESSION:

Expected postsurgical changes status post double lung transplant.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Lung transplant is performed for end-stage bilateral lung disease (e.g. CF, fibrosis, COPD, LAM, etc). This case shows the expected immediate post-operative appearance.

Expected Appearance:

  • Clamshell (Transverse) Sternotomy - performed for double lung transplant for bilateral / wide exposure
  • Support and monitoring devices (ETT, NG, central lines, chest tubes, mediastinal/pleural drains)
  • Small pneumothoraces or pneumomediastinum normal
  • Small effusions common, often resolve in < 2 weeks
  • Reperfusion edema is expected in most patients in the first 72h (noncardiogenic edema) - appears as patchy perihilar / basilar opacities, peribronchial thickening, dependent predominance.

What to look for:

  • Mispositioned lines/tubes
  • Worsening or non-resolving opacities
  • Large or increasing pleural effusion
  • New / developing opacities after early post-op period
  • Persistent pneumomediastinum, new/persistent air leak.
  • PE (on CT)

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 36 - Pulmonary Edema (Interstitial and Alveolar Edema)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Cardiomegaly with left ventricular and left atrial enlargement.
  • Vascular redistribution
  • Interstitial edema (too many lines/Kerley B lines, peribronchial thickening). Split the lung into thirds, and notice that there are too many lines too peripherally.
  • Bilateral peripheral consolidation (arrows to more confluent homogeneous opacities) consistent with alveolar edema. There is fluid tracking along both oblique fissures.

Other Findings:

  • A left subclavian vascular stent is again noted with a mid stent stenosis of approximately 50% (notice narrowing centrally)
  • Right hemodialysis catheter with distal ends in the lower superior vena cava. Most hemodialysis catheters are large bore, dual lumen, with split tips. You can see this clearly on the lateral view here.

IMPRESSION:

Cardiogenic pulmonary edema with both interstitial and alveolar edema.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: We introduced CHF and pulmonary edema in the prior cases. This case demonstrates more clear interstitial edema than prior cases, and more confluent consolidations that indicate alveolar edema.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 15 - No CHF. Prior right upper lobectomy with juxtaphrenic peaking

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Post-right upper lobectomy. There is associated right hemithorax volume loss (compare right lung vs left), juxtaphrenic peaking of the right hemidiaphragm, and mild elevation of the right hilum (single arrow).

Other Findings:

  • Patchy opacity peripheral aspect left mid lung (double arrow) corresponds to pleural calcification, demonstrated on a prior CT.
  • No focal consolidation.
  • Mediastinal contours are within normal limits.
  • No pleural effusion.

IMPRESSION:

  • No evidence of congestive heart failure.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We show this case to demonstrate the appearance post right upper lobectomy -- and see the related findings of volume loss. Here, we see the right hemithorax looks smaller and the volume loss results in: mediastinum and hilum shift rightward/upward, right hemidiaphragm elevates, and the remaining lobes move upward.

Juxtaphrenic peak refers to the small triangular “peak” on the elevated hemidiaphragm caused by traction along an accessory fissure due to upper lobe volume loss. We also saw this in a prior case of right upper lobe collapse due to an obstructing mass. Juxtaphrenic peak is common after upper lobectomy (≈40–72% after RUL, increasing over weeks) and specifically indicates upper-lobe volume loss... not seen after lower lobectomy.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Dextrocardia - the heart is located in the right hemithorax with apex pointing to the right
  • Stomach bubble is in the right upper quadrant
  • Left upper lobe collapse, with appearance of the collapse indicative of a left sided minor fissure (indicating situs inversus). This is likely related to mucus plugging in this setting.

IMPRESSION:

Dextrocardia and situs inversus. Correlate with history regarding primary ciliary dyskinesia.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Dextrocardia refers to the heart located in right hemithorax with apex pointing to the right (not just shifted right). This is distinct from "situs" - which refers to arrangement of organs, atria, bronchial tree. Dextrocardia can occur with:

  • Situs inversus (mirror image thoracic and abdominal organs)
  • Situs solitus (normal situs)
  • Situs ambiguous / heterotaxy syndromes = complex assocaited anomalies.

This is something you will cover in greater detail in your paeds block. It is determined by: atrial morphology, bronchial morphology, organ sidedness. Stomach bubble in right upper quadrant suggests situs inversus, but can be misleading / seen in heterotaxy as well.


Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD) is a ciliopathy -- ineffetive ciliary beating impairs mucociliary clearance everywhere (airways, sinuses, etc). This can lead to neonatal respiratory distress, chronic sinusitis, recurrent infections / bronchiectasis, fertility issues. In development, poor ciliary function leads to randomization of laterality (about 50% have organ laterality abnormalities).


Patients with PCD + situs inversus = "Kartagener syndrome" (Triad of situs inversus, chronic sinusitis, and bronchiectasis).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Expected Changes, Medical devices (labelled)

  • Sternotomy wires intact (post median sternotomy), surgical clips
  • Right internal jugular sheath and a pulmonary artery catheter to the level of the main pulmonary artery trunk
  • Mediastinal drains
  • Left chest tube
  • Temporary pacing wires (epicardial pacing wires often placed during CABG, removed 3-4 days post-op; double arrows here)

Other Findings:

  • Cardiopericardial silhouette is satisfactory
  • Small amount of pneumomediastinum postoperatively.
  • Left basal lung atelectasis.
  • Mild blunting of the left costophrenic angle favouring a small left basal pleural effusion. (often see small pleural effusions post-op, L>R)
  • There is no evidence of edema.
  • No pneumothorax.

Findings demonstrate expected post-CABG findings.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
You will see Xrays of patients post CABG and need to know what the expected findings are and what to look for. Expected findings include:

  • Median sternotomy --> sternotomy wires, surgical clips over the mediastinum
  • Mediastinal and chest tubes
  • Central lines and/or monitoring devices
  • ETT, enteric tube
  • Epicardial pacing wires
  • Common additional findings: small effusions, basilar atelectasis, small pneumothorax / pneumomediastinum / subQ emphysema

What to look for:

  • Malpositioned Lines / Tubes. There are many, so you need to go through them one by one. Sternotomy wire fracture too.
  • Large pneumothorax or hemothorax
  • Change over time (increasing pneumo, effusion, etc)
  • Pulmonary edema, Airspace disease
  • Mediastinal widening

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Interval enlargement of the cardiac silhouette, moderately enlarged.
  • On lateral view, there is an 'oreo cookie sign', with: low attenuation anteriorly (paracardial fat), higher attenuation fluid behind that, and lower attenuation epicardial fat behind the fluid.
  • Bilateral pleural effusions (right costophrenic angle on frontal, left on lateral)
  • Associated atelectasis.

Findings strongly suggest development of a pericardial effusion. Suggest echocardiogram to better assess.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced pericardial effusion in the prior case. As mentioned, CXR is not sensitive or specific for this, but Xrays are frequent -- and there are a few classic situations where you will call it on Xrays:

  • Short interval enlargement (present in this case but prior not shown; shown in prior case)
  • Water-bottle shaped heart (globular, smooth, featureless) - shown on prior case
  • Oreo cookie sign on lateral with pericardial fluid seen between layers of paracardial fat (anterior to fluid) and epicardial fat (posterior to fluid)

We show this case largely to show this sign on lateral. A pericardial effusion was confirmed at echo.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Faint left perihilar patchy opacity overlying the anterior left third rib (compare to prior study)
  • Trace left pleural effusion.
  • No pneumothorax.
  • Evidence of right mastectomy and right axillary dissection.

IMPRESSION:

Faint left perihilar opacity is new from prior, along with trace left pleural effusion, compatible with pneumonia.

This ended up being caused by an atypical organism (Mycoplasma Pneumonia).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We show this case to show a more subtle pneumonia. Comparing to prior helps us pick this one up.

The term "Atypical Pneumonia" is an imprecise term that has historically referred to non-lobar pneumonias caused by atypical organisms.

The imaging appearance may be less 'typical' of a lobar pneumonia -- can be bronchocentric, interstitial pattern, more ground glass, or other.

Organisms: Typical and Atypical

Typical organisms include:

  • Strep Pneumo
  • H Influenza
  • Staph Aureus

Atypical pathogens:

  • Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella
  • Viral

Importantly, imaging pattern is not really 'specific' to an organism. Still, when atypical pattern can mention to consider atypical organisms.

This case was a Mycoplasma Pneumonia.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Rotated film. Normally, the medial clavicles (arrows) should be equal distance from spinous process. Here, spinous process is closer to the right clavicle, indicating rotation to the right.
  • Left costophrenic angle and the upper abdomen are not included
  • Lungs are clear.
  • No pneumothorax. No pleural effusion (lateral)
  • Asymmetry of the hila is likely related to the rotated film. No cardiomegaly.

Other findings:

  • Prior left axillary lymph node dissection (see clips), and likely left mastectomy.

IMPRESSION:

  • No findings of pneumonia.
  • Asymmetry of the hila is likely related to film rotation.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Below we talk about film rotation, and then give you a practical quality checklist.

What is a “rotated” chest X-ray?

A rotated CXR means the patient wasn’t square to the detector, turned a bit left or right. Oblique position distorts normal symmetry and can mimic disease or hide real findings.

How to tell if it’s rotated

  • Clavicles vs spinous process: The medial ends of the clavicles should be equally distant from the spinous process. If not equal, it’s rotated toward the side where the clavicle is closer to the spine

Impact on appearance:

  • Side-to-side density: Rotation often makes one hemithorax look darker and the other whiter (darker on the side rotated away) -- notice here that the left lung is darker than the right
  • Mediastinum/heart: Rotation can artifactually shift or widen the mediastinum

Takeaway: Always assess rotation first before interpreting the lungs and mediastinum

Clinical Consequences:

  • Rotation can falsely suggest pneumothorax (darker side), pleural effusion or consolidation (whiter side), hilar fullness, or mediastinal widening/shifts
  • Affects measurements and lines/tubes

Bottom line: If clearly rotated, be cautious with asymmetries and, when possible, repeat or correlate with prior/other views

teachingSections.name: Practical Quality Checklist
teachingSections.html:
As a trainee, I always hated that 'approaches' would start with 'Quality'. While many things taught are not conscious practical checks with most modern PACS (i.e. "is this the correct patient?"), there are a few high yield ones you need to know that have real consequences:

  1. PA vs AP: AP magnifies the heart (heart further from detector), impacts interpretation. Always make note of this.
  2. Rotation (this case): Notice if its rotated -- impacts appearance of hila, mediastinum, lungs as mentioned
  3. Inspiration: About 10 posterior ribs should be visible. Under-inspiration can mimic edema (crowded vessels) and overestimate heart size
  4. Penetration (how much xray beam exposes the detector): Normally, you should be able to see the spine through the heart and see pulmonary vessels without lungs looking washed out.
  5. UNDERpenetration (too little exposure) = film too white / don't see the spine; can hide pathology and mimic findings;
  6. OVERpenetration (too much exposure) = film too dark / lung markings diminished. Can mask edema, pneumothorax, etc.
  7. Anatomic coverage: While you don't mention or think about this consciously often, obviously if important anatomy is cutoff should mention it (i.e. costophrenic angles, apices). It should cover apices to costophrenic angles, and both lateral chest walls.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Previous right bronchial stent has migrated and now lies within the lower trachea (compare current position in trachea vs prior position in right mainstem, better seen on line view)

Other findings:

  • Status post clamshell sternotomy with intact sternotomy wires.
  • Cardiomediastinal silhouette is unchanged.
  • No other change

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Bronchial stents are usually placed to treat obstruction from malignancy.

What to look for on Xray:

  • Location/orientation -- compare to prior to detect migration (like in this case)
  • Patency - see if stenotic contour, increased density within (mucus, granulation tissue), etc. Look for downstream lung collapse from obstruction
  • Other complications: stent fracture, mediastinal widening/hemorrhage (rare)

Migration is common (broad range from 5-50% reported), with risk higher if there is size mismatch, cough, or smooth short segment stent. Report degree of movement, and any complications such as repeat obstruction (not present here). Generally, bronchoscopy is performed to reposition or remove if needed.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Mild haziness obscuring left heart border. This is seen as slight increase in opacity on the lateral film. This relates to consolidation or atelectasis in the lingula.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
  • Mediastinum and cardiac silhouette are within normal limits.
  • No destructive bone lesion.

IMPRESSION:

Haziness obscuring left border of the heart, representing consolidation/atelectasis in the lingula. Pneumonia should be considered in the appropriate clinical setting.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: This case shows relatively subtle haziness in the lingula. The key here is there is silhouetting of the left heart border where we would expect the lingula, and there is a correlate in that location on the lateral. Silhouette sign was introduced in a prior case.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 65 - Tracheostomy tube (contacting right tracheal wall)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tracheostomy tube tip about 6.5 cm above carina, possibly in contact with right lateral tracheal wall (single arrow).
  • Pneumomediastinum and subcutaneous emphysema.
  • Moderate right pneumothorax (see label and arrows along pleural line, with no lung markings peripheral to that line)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A Tracheostomy tube is a short tube placed into the trachea through a surgical opening in the anterior neck for prolonged ventilation. We show this case just so that you know what it looks like.

Tip position should be in mid to upper trachea, below the upper tube and above the carina. Ideally, it should align with the middle of the air column. In this case, the right aspect abuts the right tracheal wall. Eccentric tips contacting the wall could result in injury, ulceration if chronic. But, this may be positional / due to neck position, and does not necessarily require adjustment.

There is also a right pneumothorax (see pleural line with no lung markings beyond) and pneumomediastinum. These are covered in other cases in greater detail.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate sized opacity silhouetting the right cardiac border with smooth lateral margin and obtuse angles with the lung.
  • Windowing demonstrates an ovoid, homogeneous opacity at the right cardiophrenic angle, obscuring right heart border.

This is the classic appearance of a pericardial cyst. There is a differential, covered in the learning material section.


CT was performed and confirmed that this was a pericardial cyst:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pericardial Cyst is a benign congenital fluid filled cyst arising from pericardium, most often an incidental finding. The most common location is right cardiophrenic angle (like our case). Large ones can cause compressive symptoms, but this is rare.

Classic CXR Appearance:

  • Well defined, round/oval, homogeneous opacity at the cardiphrenic angle
  • Often silhouettes or obscures the right heart border; or mimics a lateralized right heart border.
  • CXR can suggest the diagnosis, but CT required to confirm / differentiate from other pathologies.

Ddx on CXR:

  • Pericardial prominent fat pad / lipoma - fat attenuation (easier to see at CT)
  • Pericardial diverticulum - communicates with pericardium (identical appearance on CXR)
  • Other mediastinal masses (i.e. anterior or middle, extending)
  • Morgagni hernia (similar location, may mimic if no bowel gas within)
  • Chamber enlargement

CT will better depict cystic attenuation, shape, location, thin wall, without solid components or septa.


Asymptomatic cysts are observed (i.e. no intervention is required unless symptomatic or enlarging).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 27 - Sarcoidosis (bilateral hilar, right paratracheal adenopathy)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.
  • On frontal Xray, both hila are more dense and larger than normal (circles), and more nodular / lumpy bumpy (rather than smooth).
  • On Lateral, there are abnormal bulges surrounding central airways (doughnut sign), with nodular densities surrounding the airways. This includes too much opacity inferiorly where generally do not have opacity (see intro video. double arrow).
  • Right paratracheal thickening. Normally, right paratracheal stripe [density between air in trachea and lung = tracheal wall, pleura] should measure < 4 mm.
  • Bilateral lungs show small scattered hazy pulmonary opacities (arrows)
  • Pleural surfaces remain clear.

IMPRESSION:

Right paratracheal and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. These are consistent with known history of sarcoid but are new/increased from prior, all likely related to sarcoidosis. CT suggested.


In addition to knowing the classic appearance of Sarcoidosis on Xray, focus on two core skills: identifying hilar lymphadenopathy and right paratracheal stripe thickening (see below).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Sarcoidosis is an inflammatory disease that most commonly involves the lungs and chest nodes. You should know the classic appearance on chest Xray:

  • Mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy. Hilar adenopathy is typically bilateral and symmetric
  • Variable parenchymal disease (hallmark is perilymphatic nodules and septal thickening with an upper lobe predominance, best seen at CT; may see larger nodules / coalescent upper lobe masses, fibrosis in later stages). Note that the lung findings are less specific on Xray and CT is best at characterizing.

Garland Triad (1-2-3 sign)

  • This refers to the typical nodal enlargement pattern seen in sarcoidosis: 1 (right paratracheal), 2 (right hilar), and 3 (left hilar)
  • Though most classic for Sarcoid, of course other things can cause adenopathy in a similar pattern on Xray (infection, malignancy), but

Scadding Stages are a classification system on Xray with prognostic relevance that we don't report, but worth knowing to be aware of appearances. Importantly, they do not represent the sequential progression of disease.

  • Stage 0: Normal chest radiograph.
  • Stage I: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL), often symmetric
  • Stage II: BHL + parenchymal infiltrates
  • Stage III: Parenchymal infiltrates without BHL
  • Stage IV: Pulmonary fibrosis with volume loss, architectural distortion, and upper-lobe–predominant scarring

Generally higher stage has lower likelihood of spontaneous remission, but CT better at assessment.

teachingSections.name: Core Skill: Right Paratracheal Stripe
teachingSections.html:
The paratracheal stripe is the density between the gas in the trachea and right lung, normally consisting of tracheal wall, pleura, mediastinal fat. It is normally < 4 mm.

Compare our Xray (thickened stripe) with normal frontal below (red arrow = thin paratracheal stripe < 4 mm)

Thickened Paratracheal Stripe:

  • Lymphadenopathy (like this case)
  • Primary malignancy in neck (thyroid, parathyroid, tracheal, etc)
  • Pleural effusion

teachingSections.name: Core Skill: Hilar Adenopathy
teachingSections.html:
Differentiating normal hilar vessels from adenopathy can be challenging, but is an important skill on both frontal and lateral CXR.

Compare the hila in our case vs the normal case below (orange arrows = normal hilar vessels with smooth margins). Our case has increased density, size, and a lumpy bumpy appearance in comparison.


As covered in the intro video, also compare the lateral Xray in our case to a normal below (orange arrows = normal pulmonary arteries, green circle = relative paucity of density at this site normally). Compare with the findings labelled in our case.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Subcutaneous emphysema (see arrows in neck)
  • Pneumomediastinum (linear / curvilinear lucencies in the mediastinum that outline mediastinal structures [see frontal and lateral])
  • On lateral images, notice gas surrounding the right pulmonary artery ('ring around the artery sign' - see article). There are many other named signs described in learning materia.
  • Opacity in the left lower lung.
  • Moderate left pleural effusion, small right pleural effusion.

IMPRESSION:

Pneumomediastinum. Left lower lung findings could indicate pneumonia, but in this setting, an esophageal rupture should also be considered. CT Chest suggested to better assess.


Esophageal rupture was confirmed at CT.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pneumomediastinum refers to extraluminal gas in the mediastinum. Gas can come from inside (esophagus, bronchi, lung), outside (trauma, surgery), or infection. Know the appearances on CXR and causes.

CXR Appearance:

  • Lucent streaks / bubbles outlining mediastinal structures (may be more subtle 'sharpening' of contours). Named signs based on what the gas is outlining:
  • Ring-around-the-artery sign
  • Continuous diaphragm sign
  • Tubular artery sign (outlining artery branches of aorta)
  • Double bronchial wall (air outlining both sides of bronchi)
  • On lateral CXR, look for retrosternal lucencies (like in our case)
  • Subcutaneous emphysema, often in neck (gas tracking from mediastinum upwards)
  • Frank streaky mediastinal gas if large volume

Fundamental Skill: Pneumomediastinum vs Pneumopericardium vs Pneumothorax

  • Pneumopericardium (within pericardium only) = gas outlines the heart. Pericardial reflection is at great vessels, so gas will not extend above the aortic root (i.e. no gas in upper mediastinum or neck)
  • Pneumothorax - gas within pleural space. No 'streaky' opacities like seen in pneumomediastinum. We covered cases of pneumothorax separately
  • Pneumomediastinum (this case) - streaky lucencies outlining mediastinal structures, often extending up into the neck, sharpened borders due to gas.

Of course, these can coexist, especially in trauma. CT obviously better defines location of gas, but the above are fundamental things you need to know!

teachingSections.name: Ddx: Pneumomediastinum
teachingSections.html:
• Macklin Effect - spontaneous / pressure related (alveolar rupture from pressure changes --> air tracks centrally along bronchovascular into mediastinum)
  • Iatgrogenic (ventilation, vascular access, endoscopy, etc)
  • Trauma (including tracheobronchial injury)
  • Esophageal perforation - emesis-induced (Boerhaave) or iatrogenic at endoscopy
  • Infection (gas-forming in mediastinum or tracking from neck).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large mass overlying the mid and lower right lung with partial silhouetting of right heart border / SVC.
  • Hilar vessels are seen through the mass ('hilum overlay sign' - which indicates the mass is not hilar)
  • Loss of retrosternal clear space on lateral view (opacified)
  • Preserved posterior mediastinal lines

Other Findings:

  • No consolidation.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax

IMPRESSION:

Large anterior mediastinal mass extending to the right.


This ended up being a thymoma, which is the most common primary neoplasm of anterior mediastinum. Thymomas are associated with several conditions, most importantly Myasthenia Gravis.

Typical appearance on CXR is a well-defined soft tissue mass in anterior mediastinum lobulated to one side.

teachingSections.name: Approach to Mediastinal Masses
teachingSections.html:
Mediastinal Masses are masses that arise from the mediastinum. Identifying, localizing, and characterizing the mass on CXR, along with giving a differential, is a fundamental skill you need to have. We will review anterior, middle, and posterior mediastinal masses through a handful of cases.

The first steps are to determine if it is a mediastinal mass, and then localize to a compartment -- which helps narrow the differential significantly.

Is it a Mediastinal Mass?

  • Often (but not always) creates obtuse angles with mediastinum
  • Will not have air bronchograms
  • Disrupts mediastinal lines and structures

Which Compartment?

  • Anterior Mediastinum (This Case, anterior to trachea)
  • Middle Mediastinum
  • Posterior Mediastinum (> 1 cm posterior to the anterior border of the vertebral bodies)

We cover Anterior mediastinal masses (this case) in the section below

teachingSections.name: Anterior Mediastinal Masses
teachingSections.html:
Know how to localize to anterior mediastinum and the classic differential.

Signs of Anterior Med Mass:

  • Hilum Overlay Sign - can see the hilum through the mass (indicating it is not hilar, and likely anterior or less likely posterior)
  • Retrosternal clear space (lateral) - opacified (normally hypoattenuating fat)
  • Silhouette Sign - silhouetting of the right heart border / SVC border (which sits anteriorly) indicates it contacts these structures, meaning anterior
  • Posterior mediastinal lines seen as normal (R/L paraspinal lines)
  • Anterior Junction Line Displaced - the anterior junction line is where the lungs appose anteriorly behind the upper sternum (see the normal appearance from this article). Displacement or abnormal convexity can occur with anterior mediastinal abnormality (but may also not be seen in normal cases too).

As an aside: the Posterior Junction Line is where the lungs meet posteriorly. This is higher up and extends above the clavicles. There is a sign called "Cervicothoracic sign"

Classic Differential (MUST Know):

You'll see the "4 T's" described in different ways. Use the one below to cover your bases:

  1. Thymus Related (including Thymoma [this case], thymic carcinoma, etc)
  2. Thyroid related (goitre, cancer, etc)
  3. Teratoma and other Germ Cell Tumors
  4. Terrible Lymphoma

(Some will add Thoracic Aorta as a 5th T to account for vascular masses).

teachingSections.name: Lines/Stripes on CXR: Important
teachingSections.html:
This ECR article has excellent images correlating CXR lines with CT images, most importantly:

  • Anterior Junction Line
  • Posterior Junction Line
  • Right and Left Paratracheal Stripes
  • AP Window and Stripe
  • Azygoesophageal Recess
  • Paraspinal Lines and paraaortic line posteriorly

You need to know these, so take the time to get used to what normal looks like and compare with abnormal examples in this article, and in this course.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Widened superior mediastinum and abnormal mediastinal contours (arrows), with dilated sigmoid shaped esophagus (arrows)
  • Esophagus is food filled (mottled appearance) with an air-fluid level
  • Lateral projection shows that the dilated structure behind the trachea extending down to GE junction

IMPRESSION:

Severely dilated esophagus filled with mottled intraluminal material, with an air-fluid level. Findings can be seen with achalasia, but requires further evaluation to assess for other obstructing cause (i.e. malignancy).


This patient had confirmed achalasia.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Achalasia is an esophageal dysmotility disorder that involves failure of relaxation of lower esophageal sphincter and absent peristalsis of the esophagus. The result is functional outflow obstruction that results in a very dilated esophagus with a 'birds beak' appearance. Patients present with dysphagia, regurgitation of food, weight loss. Diagnosis is made via a combination of manometry (motility test), barium swallow, and scope to exclude underlying malignancy.


The main reason to include it in this CXR course is to demonstrate the appearance of a very dilated esophagus on standard CXR, which should be recognized so that the patient can be evaluated. CXR demonstrates a very dilated esophagus, but does not confirm the diagnosis.

Ddx at this point includes:

  • Achalasia (motility issue)
  • Pseudoachalasia (dilated esophagus, but due to obstructing malignancy)
  • Other causes (scleroderoma, chagas disease, esophageal stricture)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Normal cardiac silhouette.
  • No consolidation or atelectasis.
  • No pulmonary masses or nodules.
  • No pleural effusions.
  • Incidental note of Azygos fissure (arrows)

IMPRESSION:

Normal chest radiograph.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
There are no acute findings in this case. We show this case to intersperse some Normal cases to keep you honest... but also to show you a normal variant (Azygos Lobe).

Azygos Lobe is a normal variant (1%) that occurs when a precursor to azygos vein penetrates lung during development from superior to inferior and takes pleural covering with it. The result is two double layers of pleura (4 layers total) invaginating from right upper lobe down to the azygos vein (which is encased by pleura).

The "Azygos Lobe" is the part of the RUL medial to the invagination. The azygos fissure is well seen.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right cardiophrenic angle opacity obscuring right heart border with leftward deviation of the heart
  • Lateral images confirm anterior
  • Gas-filled tubular structures within (bowel loops)

Other Findings:

  • Left ventricular enlargement
  • Atherosclerotic calcifications of aorta
  • No consolidation or pulmonary edema.
  • No pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Findings are in keeping with a large right Morgagni hernia. CT can be considered to confirm and to evaluate for complications, as clinically indicated.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Morgagni Hernia is an anterior congenital diaphragmatic defect at sternocostal (parasternal) region. It happens at the foramen of Morgagni -- a developmental gap where superior epigastric vessels pass, with failure to close causing the defect. It is usually right sided (heart protects left). It is often incidental in adults, but can present after increase in intraabdominal pressure.


CXR Findings:

  • Right (or left) cardiophrenic angle opacity, often well defined
  • If contains hollow viscus (transverse colon for example), will see air filled bowel loops like in our case +/- air-fluid levels
  • May see: elevated diaphragm, mediastinal shift, basal atelectasis

Ddx on Xray depends on appearance but broad if no bowel herniates (more nonspecific on CXR), including: pericardial cyst, lymphadenopathy, prominent epicardial fat pad, etc. If gas is present like in our case, the diagnosis is more obvious.


CT is performed to confirm, identify contents of hernia, and look for complications (obstruction, ischemia, etc). Also helps with surgical planning.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 13 - Right basal pneumonia, pleural effusion

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right central line with its tip in the right atrium. Left PICC line with its tip in the right cavoatrial junction.
  • Enlarged cardiopericardial silhouette, even allowing for AP projection (Portable).
  • Ill-defined opacity silhouetting the right hemidiaphragm suggestive of right basal atelectasis/consolidation and associated small right pleural effusion.
  • No pneumothorax.
  • No aggressive bony lesion.

IMPRESSION:

Likely right basal pneumonia and associated pleural effusion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates a right basal consolidation with associated right pleural effusion.

We see silhouetting of the right diaphragm (cannot see the right diaphragm), and the more homogeneous high attenuation at the right lung base blunts the costophrenic angle with a meniscus sign (effusion). Just above that, we see increased opacity in lung tissue (consolidation). These are labelled.

Another clue of an effusion being present in addition to consolidation is to look for mediastinal shift away in the setting of larger effusions.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 42 - LA enlargement, Mitral Valve Prosthesis

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Cardiomegaly (Cardiothoracic ratio >> 0.5)

Marked left atrial enlargement.

  • Double density at right heart border. In this case the LA border actually extends beyond the right heart border due to severe enlargement
  • Measurement from mid left mainstem bronchus to LA border is >> 7 cm
  • Splaying of carina (> 90 degree bifurcation)

Bi-leaflet mitral valve prosthesis, in expected location on frontal and lateral.

IMPRESSION:

Severe left atrial enlargement.


This patient had a history of mitral regurgitation, causing massive LA enlargement. Mitral valve was replaced, but consequences (LA enlargement) still seen.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced these signs of LA enlargement in a prior case, which are reviewed below. In this case, the right border of the LA extends further lateral than the RA border. The RA border will be continuous with the SVC (small arrows).

Below we also introduce valve replacement / repair.

teachingSections.name: LA Enlargement on CXR:
teachingSections.html:
In addition to identifying cardiomegaly (CTR > 0.5), there are signs of specific chamber enlargement (LA mostly) on Xray that are classic exam questions. Echo, CT, and MRI are obviously much better at this, but Xrays are performed frequently and so it's something you need to know for real life practice too.

Left Atrial Enlargement:

LA sits posteriorly, so on frontal Xray is not normally seen (left heart border is mostly LV, with LA appendage superiorly). With enlargement:

  • Double Density Sign - right side of LA becomes visible either behind RA or beyond right heart border.
  • Most reliable sign: With double density, if measurement from midpoint of left mainstem bronchus to right border of LA is > 7 cm (here it is much larger)
  • Splaying of Carina with bifurcation angle > 90 degrees (here it is > 90 degrees)

teachingSections.name: Valve Replacement
teachingSections.html:
Heart Valves (Mitral, Tricuspid, Pulmonary, Aortic) may require replacement or repair when diseased (i.e. stenosis, regurgitation) and severe or symptomatic. Echo, MR, CT performed for assessment, but will see on Xray frequently (like this case) and so need to know their expected locations (classic exam question) and appearance. Mitral and Aortic are the most commonly replaced.

Types of Valves:

  • Mechanical (synthetic material) - high longevity, require anticoagulation
  • Bioprosthetic (from animal valves) - no anticoagulation needed, limited durability / longevity

Anatomy (Most Important):

I've drawn on circles at the expected locations of each valve on the frontal and lateral.

On Lateral:

  • Drawing a line from apex to base of heart (see oblique line), the mitral and tricuspid valves are below this line and the aortic and pulmonary above.
  • Pulmonary valve most anterior
  • Mitral valve most posterior

On Frontal:

  • Ascending aorta is oriented up and to the right, so aortic valve is oriented that way
  • Pulmonary trunk oriented upwards / slightly left.
  • Tricuspid quite inferior / anterior (right heart sits anterior)

See this diagram from radiology assistant, and check out this part of the article (high yield = the anatomy).


Also see this case in a patient with all four valves published here.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Interval development of complete whiteout of the left lung with volume loss represented by leftward mediastinal shift and elevation of the left hemidiaphragm (window hard to see this) most consistent with collapse.

IMPRESSION:

White out of the left lung is most consistent with collapse, most likely secondary to endobronchial mucus plug given short interval development. Clinical correlation and follow-up radiograph recommended.

Here is the radiograph from 2 days prior:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Complete whiteout of a hemithorax is a classic scenario on exams and in real life, so you need a basic approach to this.

Determine if lung volume is decreased (mediastinal shift towards), increased (mediastinal shift away), or normal (no shift)

Decreased Volume:

  • **Total lung collapse (this case)
  • Pneumonectomy

Increased Volume:

  • **Large pleural effusion (most common), or hemothorax
  • Other less common: diaphragmatic hernia, large mass

Normal volume:

  • **Diffuse consolidation (infection, unilateral pulmonary edema, etc)


Complete Lung Collapse:

Like lobar collapse, consider:

  • Mucus plug / endobronchial infection or blood (if acutely developed, like this case)
  • Neoplasm / malignancy
  • Foreign body aspiration
  • Endobronchial intubation (showed a case earlier)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Prior right mastectomy (arrows), with absent right breast shadow.
  • Resultant apparent asymmetry of lower lungs, mimicking increased opacity on left (or reduced on right).
  • No focal consolidation
  • No other acute findings

IMPRESSION:

No focal consolidation. Prior right mastectomy.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Absent right breast shadow after mastectomy reduces overlying soft-tissue attenuation, creating apparent asymmetry on PA CXR. This can falsely suggest increased opacity in the contralateral (left) lower lung or “lucency” on the right. Recognize the missing breast silhouette before calling consolidation.


Pearls beyond the basics above:

  • Use lateral if unsure about subtle true opacity in these patients (i.e. spine sign)
  • Will often see associated surgical clips, chest wall asymmetry.
  • Look for other sequelae of prior breast cancer: i.e. fibrosis from prior supraclav radiation, or findings of tumor spread (i.e. bone lesion, lung nodules, etc)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Small to moderate right-sided pneumothorax. Notice pleural line (arrows) without any lung markings peripheral to this.
  • Expiratory Views make the pneumothorax even more apparent, with the same findings (pleural line with smaller arrows, no lung markings peripheral to it)

Other Notes (pertinent negatives):

  • No mediastinal shift.
  • No focal consolidation, pleural effusion or pulmonary edema.
  • Cardiopericardial silhouette is within normal limits.

IMPRESSION:

Small to moderate right pneumothorax.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pneumothorax refers to air in the pleural space, causing collapse of the lung.

On CXR, look for a pleural line with no lung markings peripheral to the line. But, a few additional things to know:

  • Upright films are more sensitive (air rises to apex)
  • Supine films can be more subtle as gas collects anteriorly/inferomedial -- classic signs include deep sulcus sign (lucent / deep CPA)
  • CT obviously more sensitive
  • Don't confuse normal interfaces for pleural line (i.e. medial scapula, rib margins, skin folds etc).
  • Always look for tension (i.e. mediastinal shift)

Inspiratory and Expiratory views

  • Expiration can make the pneumothorax more apparent (reduces lung volume / lung more dense = may see it better).
  • Thus, expiratory views can be helpful when inspiratory film is equivocal for a small apical pneumothorax.
  • Routine use of expiratory views is NOT necessary. Start with a good inspiratory PA

cases.clinicalHistory: Incidental bilateral AMLs, nonspecific chest sounds

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Abnormal lungs, with reticular appearing abnormality (too many lines)
  • In retrospect, you can see rounded areas of lucency, which later were found to be lung cysts (see CT below)

Other findings:

  • Small pleural effusions, right greater than left, with associated atelectasis

IMPRESSION:

Abnormal CXR with increased reticulation in both lungs and bilateral pleural effusions.

CT was later performed and demonstrated bilateral uniform round lung cysts throughout, including involvement of the CPAs, typical of LAM:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
LAM (Lymphangioleiomyomatosis) is a rare cystic lung disease, involving abnormal proliferation that obstructs airways and forms thin walled lung cysts. It can be sporadic or associated with Tuberous Sclerosis. We cover this in more detail in the CT course - including the typical findings:

  • Numerous uniform round thin walled cysts throughout the lung (no sparing of CPA)
  • May have chylous effusions, pneumothorax
  • May have abdominal involvement
  • May have findings of TSC

Recall that LCH (Langerhans) has irregular cysts that tend to spare costophrenic recess; associated with smoking.


CXR on the other hand is much more nonspecific -- as lung cysts are hard to see (like in our case). But, Xray is often abnormal:

  • Generalized symmetric reticular / reticulonodular pattern (may see visible cysts in advanced disease)
  • May see complications: chylous effusions, pneumothorax
  • Early on, CXR may be normal

In short, CXR findings overlap with other interstitial lung diseases and cystic lung disease, and CT is required to characterize! In this case, recognize that the CXR is abnormal and suggest CT for further characterization.


Why a reticular pattern here on CXR?

  • Innumerable cysts create interfaces (and mild interstitial thickening can occur), which can create a "mesh-like appearance" on CXR -- mimicking reticulation despite absence of true fibrosis
  • CT clarifies / clearly identifies the lung cysts as demonstrated

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bilateral upper, mid and lower zone opacities are high density / calcified with a holly leaf appearance (arrows) -- corresponding to calcified pleural plaques. Given high density, see them well on line view. Notice the more dense irregular edges (arrows)
  • No diaphragmatic pleural calcifications.
  • No new consolidation or pneumonia in the lungs.
  • Low lung volumes with mild basilar atelectasis, especially in the right medial lower lobe.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pleural Plaques are well-circumscribed areas of parietal pleural fibrosis/thickening that occur due to remote asbestos exposure (occur 20+ years post exposure). They are an asymptomatic marker of exposure. You should know their appearance, distribution, and understand asbestos-related lung diseases.

Appearance:

  • Multiple, bilateral sharply marginated pleural based opacities
  • May be calcified, but don't have to be (many are not!)
  • Thickened / rolled / nodular edges give the Holly-Leaf appearance when seen en-face (raised jagged edges), and the "Incomplete Border Sign" on Xray
  • CT is much more sensitive and specific. Non-calcified plaques can mimic other diseases, so remember the raised rolled edges and typical appearance/distribution!

Distribution:

  • Posterolateral and lateral chest wall, diaphragmatic, less commonly mediastinal pleura
  • Spare costophrenic angles and apices

Quick Intro: Most Important Asbestos-Related Lung Diseases

Pleural Plaques (discussed above):

  • Long latency (>20 yrs)
  • Asymptomatic marker of exposure

Asbestosis (pulmonary fibrosis)

  • Fibrotic lung disease similar to UIP (more severe = parenchymal bands, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing with a lower lobe predominance)
  • Typically 10-15 yrs post-exposure, dose related
  • Pleural plaques may or may not be present

Mesothelioma (cancer)

  • Aggressive malignant tumor of mesothelium (mostly pleural, but can be peritoneal)
  • Very long latency (often 30-40 yrs)
  • Unilateral pleural mass or nodular thickening, can be circumferential and extend into fissures; associated volume loss with progression.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Increased opacity at the left apex (arrows) with associated volume loss in the left hemithorax (mediastinal shift to left, notice juxtaphrenic peaking on frontal and lateral xray).
  • The right lung is clear.

IMPRESSION:

Appearances are suspicious for TB.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
TB (Tuberculosis) is an infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium Tuberculosis that can involve various organ systems -- most commonly the lungs (pulmonary TB). You should know the classic imaging appearances. While "primary" and "post-primary" help understand various patterns, they can overlap in reality.


Basics of Pulmonary TB:

At first time inhalation:

  • Typically, immune system contains it and forms granulomas --> Latent TB.
  • But, it can also cause acute infection ("Primary TB"). This often looks like other infections (lobar consolidation, lymphadenopathy [often asymmetric], +/- effusion).

Latent TB can "Reactivate" (Post-Primary TB). Typical appearance:

  • Upper lobe predominant patchy heterogeneous consolidation (apical / posterior); or superior segment lower lobe
  • Cavitation common
  • Volume loss / fibrosis (like our case)
  • Tree-in-bud nodules (on CT)
  • Lymphadenopathy less prominent

For Understanding / Interest: Why Upper Lobes/Apices in Post-Primary?

TB is aerobic and prefers to grow where there is more oxygen. Upper lobes / apices have higher oxygen tension (relatively over-ventilated, under-perfused). Thus, latent TB tends to preferentially show up here first after reactivation!

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Chest:

  • ETT in place
  • Complete opacification of the left hemithorax with multiple air-filled structures within (arrows), without identification of left diaphragm. Gastric bubble remains within the abdominal cavity, with orogastric tube in place.
  • Associated mediastinal and tracheal shift to the right

Abdomen:

  • Beside the gas distended stomach, a few gas-filled bowel loops seen in left upper quadrant.
  • Remainder of the abdomen is gasless.

IMPRESSION:

Findings consistent with left congenital diaphragmatic hernia with small bowel loops identified in the left hemithorax. Stomach is subdiaphragmatic.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) is a defect in the diaphragm allowing abdominal viscera to herniate into the thorax, most commonly left posterior-lateral (Bochdalek), less often anterior (Morgagni) or hiatal. We often see incidental small adult bochdalek hernias containing fat on CT.

Morbidity/mortality stem from pulmonary hypoplasia and pulmonary hypertension caused by mass effect on developing lungs; associated anomalies are common.


Classic CXR findings are shown in this case:

  • Air-filled bowel within left hemithorax (sometimes stomach with NG tube within)
  • Compressed ipsilateral lung
  • Mediastinal shift away
  • Hemidiaphragm not seen

Mimics include: pulmonary cysts, large hydropneumothorax, eventration. If uncertain can perform an upper GI contrast study or CT.

Management involves stabilization and then surgical repair.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Interval placement of a coarctation stent. < 50% narrowing of the mid coarctation stent seen on lateral.
  • Bilateral rib notching (arrows). 'Bites' out of inferior ribs posteriorly, sparing ribs 1-2; caused by collaterals (see learning materials).

Other Findings:

  • Heart not enlarged.
  • No obvious aortic valve calcification.
  • No pulmonary venous congestion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced aortic coarctation in the prior case. In this case, we demonstrate:

  • Appearance of rib notching (arrows)
  • Coarctation stent for treatment

Rib Notching:

Only seen with established collaterals (subclavian --> internal thoracic --> intercostal arteries -- pulsation causing remodeling of rib = inferior rib notching)

  • Bilateral 'bites' from inferior ribs
  • In classic post-ductal coarctation, spares ribs 1-2 (like our case, given supplied by costocervical trunk and not descending aorta), generally involves ribs 3-8

Coarctation Stent on CXR:

Look for:

  • Stent position / alignment, compare to prior
  • Stent integrity (discontinuity, fracture)
  • Configuration change (i.e. kinking, compression / narrowing, asymmetry)
  • Stenosis is best assessed via CTA / MRI, but can comment on any external contour changes (like our case) on Xray

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Tip of the ETT is 11.4 cm above the carina, too high (see labels)

Other Findings (covered in separate cases):

  • Additional medical devices are present, including an LVAD (left ventricular assist device), PA catheter (Swan Ganz), and ICD. Don't worry about these for now -- we introduce these in more detail later in the course.
  • Small left pleural effusion and left mid to lower lung zone opacification is stable.
  • Mild pulmonary venous congestion.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
ETT should ideally be 3-5 cm above the carina (many use 3-7 cm). We previously showed a case of what can happen if it's too low (right mainstem bronchus intubation). If it's too high, there is a risk of accidental extubation (unstable airway), and can injure vocal cords. Generally report as too high if > 7 cm above carina.

How to Measure:

  • Find the carina on CXR (where trachea splits).
  • Measure tip-to-carina distance: report “ETT tip is X cm above carina.”
  • “Too high” is commonly defined as ≥7 cm

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Airspace opacity (consolidation) in the left mid to lower lung with air bronchograms
  • Small left pleural effusion.
  • No pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Consolidation in the left mid to lower lung with air bronchogram, suggestive of pneumonia or aspiration in this setting.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case shows consolidation in the left lower lung (relatively homogeneous increased opacity that obscures vessels). This is a case of pneumonia.

In addition, there are air bronchograms visible, which refer to:

  • Air filled bronchi coursing through opacified lung -- which appear as dark (air filled) circles or lines "within" the area of consolidation. You may see branching lucencies.

Air bronchograms are not uncommon in consolidation. All it means is that the airways are patent at that site, supporting airspace disease (i.e. alveoli are filled with 'something' like pus [pneumonia], blood [hemorrhage], fluid [edema], or something else). They favor a non-obstructive process (i.e. with obstructing mass, usually airways are not air filled). But, you don't see them all the time in consolidation.

Here is a case with more obvious air bronchograms.

study.description: Chest routine plus AP inspiration Port

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Increase in size of the cardiopericardial silhouette compared to prior film.
  • Water bottle configuration of heart. Notice rounded boders with relatively 'featureless' borders.
  • Findings could be related to interval development of pericardial effusion

Other Findings:

  • Nasogastric tube noted, however, the distal tip is not below the diaphragm. It should be advanced by at least 10 cm.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pericardial effusion is abnormal accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac, around the heart. CXR is NOT sensitive or specific. However, there are a couple of classic presentations on Xray that you need to know:

  • Enlarged, globular, 'water bottle' shaped heart (smooth, globular, symmetric enlargement due to fluid accumulation -- the normal contours become rounded and featureless)
  • Short interval increase in size of cardiac silhouette
  • Lateral view: oreo cookie sign (fluid between epicardial and pericardial fat) - specific but not sensitive

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 43 - Cardiac Conduction Device Lead Fracture

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Medical Devices:

  • Left sided cardiac conduction device (see learning material for types) with leads projecting over the right atrium, right ventricle and coronary sinus (tips labelled).
  • Focal discontinuity (zoom in to above generator) of the pacer lead that travels through the coronary sinus. This discontinuity is located approximately 8 cm from the generator. This is concerning for lead fracture.
  • The remainder of the leads are intact.

Other Findings:

  • The cardiac silhouette is enlarged with a multichamber configuration including left ventricle, right ventricle, and left atrium.
  • Extensive calcification of the bilateral carotid arteries (arrows in neck)

IMPRESSION:

Triple lead ICD with a break within the lead extending into the coronary sinus. Cardiomegaly.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Broadly, split Cardiac Conduction Devices include two major types:

  • Pacemakers - treat bradycardia by pacing.
  • ICDs (Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillators) - detect and terminate V-Tach/V-Fib with high energy shocks, can also pace.

There are only a few basic things to know that will take you ahead of most.

All of these have:

  • Pulse Generator (metal housing with battery, circuitry that senses signals via leads and delivers electrical impulses as needed)
  • Leads (insulated wires that connect from generator to heart, for sensing and delivering). Lead Tip (electrode) is uninsulated (interface with myocardium)

ICDs only will have a Defibrillator -- identified by at least one 'Shock Coil' (thicker white spring-like band) in RV +/- SVC [in this case we see both, labeled]

Differentiate Devices:

  • PACEMAKERS - single chamber (RA appendage OR RV), dual chamber (RA appendage and RV), with no defibrillator.
  • ICDs have at least one shock coil. Device can be single chamber (RV w/ coils), dual chamber (RV w/ coils and RA lead without coil).
  • Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT) refers to devices that also try to coordinate LV and RV contraction in patients with heart failure. These will have an LV lead as well, placed via coronary sinus to a lateral vein. This includes CRT-P (pacemaker only) and CRT-D (with defibrillator).

In real practice, many people just report them as cardiac conduction device or pacemakers. But, its worth understanding components.

Complications to look for:

  • Positioning - leads / tips in expected locations and chambers. Look for migration on subsequent exams. Shock coil positioning as well. Battery twisting over multiple exams (twiddler's syndrome)
  • Lead fracture (like our case) = focal discontinuity or new kinking. May be obvious or more subtle, so must trace leads.
  • Early post-op pneumothorax/ hemothorax, pneumomediastinum, lead in wrong chamber, pericardial effusion.

Practical tips:

Always trace each lead from generator to tip on both views; any shock coil = defibrillator capability (ICD); posterior/lateral venous course = LV CS (coronary sinus) lead.


In our patient, a new ICD was inserted. See follow-up below, with intact leads.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 59 - Chest Tube Malposition (Side hole in chest wall)

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Tubes/Lines:

  • Right sided chest tube, with side hole of the chest tube is located outside of the pleural space (in the chest wall). This requires repositioning / replacement.
  • Other: Endotracheal tube, NG Tube, Pulmonary Artery Catheter (Swan Ganz) present

Other findings:

  • Subcutaneous emphysema in the right lateral chest wall (arrows in right chest wall).
  • Subpulmonic component of a known right pneumothorax.
  • Sternotomy wires intact.
  • All findings stable otherwise (from prior, which is not shown here)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Chest tubes are placed to evacuate air or fluid from the pleural space. The exact ideal position of the chest tube depends on why it was placed, but the tip and side-hole should be in the pleural space at a minimum:

  • Follow radiopaque (white) stripe, and find first side-hole (gap in stripe) -- which should be medial to inner rib margin (here it is clearly lateral and in chest wall!)
  • Other common sites of malposition: subcutaneous (along chest wall), abdominal / subdiaphragmatic, mediastinal (too medial). Others better seen at CT (intraparenchymal, etc)
  • Look for obvious kinking

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Triangular retrocardiac opacity in the right lung, favoured to represent lower lobe collapse. This was not present on a recent prior.
  • Associated volume loss with slight rightward shift of mediastinum.

Other Findings:

  • Defibrillator pads are noted on the chest wall (labelled - be careful to not mistaken for disease)
  • Cardiomegaly
  • There is no significant pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Right lower lobe collapse. Enlarged atria could cause bronchial compression and resultant collapse. The differential diagnoses would include aspiration and pneumonia. Clinical correlation and follow up imaging is recommended.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Right Lower Lobe Collapse tends to form a triangular opacity from hilum to diaphragm , similar to LLL collapse (though on the left there is more overlap with the heart). The medial right diaphragm is often obscured.

Atelectasis Signs (General):

We cover classic appearances of lobar collapse, but the underlying principles are also important. In general look for:

  • Fissure displacement
  • Increased density of collapsed lobe
  • Indirect Signs (volume loss)
  • Elevated hemidiaphragm
  • Hilar displacement towards
  • Mediastinal Shift
  • Rib crowding
  • Indirect: Increased lucency of remaining ipsilateral lung

Terminology:

"Atelectasis" means collapsed lung, but is a broad term used for smaller linear areas of atelectasis (i.e. subsegmental atelectasis, band-like atelectasis, dependent atelectasis, etc) and sometimes more confluent areas.

"Collapse" is generally reserved for larger more confluent areas of collapse, like the cases of lobar collapse we are showing.

cases.clinicalHistory: Prev left upper lobectomy, prior CXR normal

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate left sided hydropneumothorax (large air-fluid level with horizontal meniscus, irregular pleural line with peripheral lucency). This was new/increased since prior normal CXR (not shown)
  • Associated mediastinal shift to the right (away from effusion)
  • Prior left upper lobectomy, with surgical clips over left upper lung
  • Small right effusion

IMPRESSION:

Left hydropneumothorax, moderate, new/increased from prior. Correlate with history and consider bronchopleural fistula given prior left lung surgery.


CT Chest (and follow-up CXR) demonstrated progressive increase, suspicious for bronchopleural fistula:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Hydropneumothorax refers to both air and fluid in pleural space -- producing a visible air-fluid level on upright film (like shown here). Assess for tension physiology with mediastinal shift away.


Bronchopleural Fistula is abnormal connection between bronchial tree and pleural space.

  • Most commonly seen post surgery (i.e. lobectomy).
  • Consider it in a post-op patient with new, progressively enlarging, or persistent pneumothorax / hydropneumothorax despite drainage.

Explain the pneumo appearance:

  • Notice that despite the very large pneumothorax obvious on CT, it is comparatively harder to see on CXR (i.e. don't see a typical pleural line with gas surrounding and a lack of lung markings peripheral to the line)
  • This is because in post-op patients, pleura is often thickened/fibrosed with adhesions, and so tethering creates pleural "compartments". Gas increases into trapped pleural compartments rather than freely around the entire lung. traps into 'compartments'

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Smooth round well-defined left sided paraspinal mass (in the posterior mediastinum), with round/mass-like thickening at paraspinal line.
  • The normal descending aorta, azygoesophageal recess (window hard) are labelled.

IMPRESSION:

Well-defined mass in the left posterior mediastinum. Differential diagnosis includes a neurogenic tumor and a benign cyst.


CT was performed that demonstrated a well defined paraspinal cyst with imperceptible walls. This was a duplication cyst.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates a posterior mediastinal mass with a paraspinal mass, as demonstrated in the key findings section. You need to be able to recognize posterior mediastinal masses, and know the classic differential. If remember one, think neurogenic tumors.


Ddx: Posterior Mediastinal Masses

  • ***Neurogenic Tumors (most common) - if remember one thing it should be this. Nerves, of course, live posterior mediastinum / paraspinal (from neuroforamen, sympathetic chain) -- so naturally, schwannomas and sympathetic tumors can live here.
  • Duplication Cysts (This Case) - foregut duplication cysts (i.e. esophageal, neurenteric, bronchogenic) = well defined cystic masses

Others:

  • Vascular - descending aorta related
  • Lymphadenopathy
  • Bone related - i.e. discitis/osteomyelitis (including TB), metastases
  • Extramedullary Hematopoeisis - often bilateral smooth paraspinal masses in patients with chronic anemia
  • Meningocele

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right paratracheal thickening suspicious for right paratracheal lymph node enlargement.
  • No hilar adenopathy
  • No intrapulmonary metastases
  • No other acute findings.

IMPRESSION:

Right paratracheal thickening is suspicious for with mediastinal lymph node enlargement. CT should be performed to confirm.


CT was performed and demonstrated metastatic lymphadenopathy (red arrows). The primary esophageal cancer is also visible on this image, not well seen on Xray.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced important lines and recesses on CXR. Here there is right paratracheal thickening in a patient with esophageal cancer. While paratracheal thickening has a differential, in this clinical setting, the first consideration should be metastatic lymphadenopathy, which was confirmed at CT.

Recall what a normal paratracheal stripe looks like. Note the normal SVC and compare to the nodular paratracheal thickening (arrows).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 38 - No findings of pneumonia; Disruption of sternotomy wire

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
PA and lateral chest radiograph findings:

  • Right chest wall pacemaker. The leads are intact, tips in right atrial appendage and right ventricle.
  • Midline sternotomy wires are noted. Disruption of the inferior-most sternotomy wire.
  • No focal consolidation or air space opacity.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
  • Cardiac silhouette is not enlarged.

No evidence of pneumonia.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case that was negative for pneumonia. We show this case to demonstrate the appearance of disruption of a sternotomy wire.

What are Sternotomy Wires?

After median sternotomy (open heart surgery), the sternum is closed with metal wires (stainless steel or titanium) that are twisted to hold the bone together while it heals.

Fractured Sternotomy Wires

Fractured sternotomy wires are not uncommon (2-3%). Most single broken wires are incidental / patients are fine (but should be reported). However, they can signal sternal instability, especially if early post-op, which can rarely lead to complications (i.e. RV / pericardial injury).

What to Report:

  • Report sternotomy wires as intact if intact
  • Look for discontinuity (wire fracture)
  • Look for change in position / alignment. Loss of alignment can also be seen with instability.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Widened mediastinum, with massive fusiform abnormality of left upper mediastinal border and absence of normal aortic knob.
  • Borders of mass likely contiguous with descending aorta (see arrows here)
  • Likely displaced calcifications along the periphery of the mass

This represents a large thoracic aortic aneurysm.

Other Findings:

  • Sternotomy wires
  • No pulmonary edema, consolidation.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

Large thoracic aortic aneurysm. CTA can be performed to confirm and better assess.


See CT follow-up, better demonstrating the aneurysm:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm (TAA) refers to enlargement of the thoracic aorta. There are different cutoffs used, but ascending aorta > 4 cm is abnormal.


CXR Findings:

  • Widened mediastinum with prominent aortic contour with a smooth / fusiform or saccular contour change; continuous with descending aorta
  • Curvilinear calcifications may be displaced outwards toward periphery of mass
  • Displacement of normal mediastinal structures / lines (tracheal deviation, obliterated AP window, left paraspinal/ paratracheal lines obliterated)

CT / CTA will much better characterize.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 21 - Organizing Pneumonia (Chronic and Fluctuating Consolidations)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Interval improvement in peripheral bilateral consolidations (compare current study and prior study)
  • Interval development / worsening of airspace disease elsewhere, including (short arrows on current)

IMPRESSION:

Interval improvement in peripheral consolidations and worsening of airspace disease elsewhere. Consider organizing pneumonia, eosinophilic pneumonia, or recurrent infection.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
