This was a case of COP (Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia). The images demonstrate chronic but improving consolidations and fluctuating / migratory consolidation. While the findings are not diagnostic of COP, they raise suspicion and we can use this opportunity to introduce how to deal with chronic consolidations and migratory consolidations.


Chronic Consolidations are those that persist / do not resolve (usually >4-6 weeks). There is a classic differential you need to know that includes:

  • Organizing Pneumonia (including COP) - often demonstrate bilateral consolidations, classic reverse halo sometimes seen on CT. The periphery is more dense consolidation and the centre more ground glass -- which you can actually see on the Xray in this case on the left (circle). It is not entirely specific but classically associated with OP.
  • Chronic Eosinophilic Pneumonia (CEP) - often peripheral upper lobe predominant consolidations
  • Neoplastic. Mainly Adenocarcinoma Spectrum lesions (adenocarcinoma in situ, mucinous adenocarcinoma; previously called BAC/bronchoalveolar carcinoma), but also other neoplasms like lymphoma can present with chronic/non-resolving consolidation. This is one reason we do follow-ups to ensure resolution in patients with high risk or atypical presentation.
  • Atypical infections (non-resolving, TB/fungal), lipoid pneumonia

These may look identical to pneumonia / other consolidations at initial presentation, but do not resolve. Though the list is longer, always think: COP, CEP, Adenocarcinoma spectrum lesion first (the classic ddx).


Migratory Consolidations refer to those that change locations / lobes between exams. There is some overlap in the ddx, with primary considerations of COP and CEP:

  • Organizing Pneumonia - hallmark of migratory consolidations with peribronchial and subpleural distribution
  • Eosinophilic lung disease - can also be migratory and transient. Check for eosinophilia and asthma history.
  • Atypical or recurrent infections
  • Vasculitis


We cover COP / organizing pneumonia more generally in the CT Chest course.

cases.clinicalHistory: Bilateral chest tubes, prior infection, f/u

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Biconvex / ovoid opacity on the right with more well defined inferior/medial border and less well defined superolateral border. This is in the expected region of the oblique fissure.
  • Bilateral small pleural effusions, right greater than left, with associated atelectasis
  • Bilateral chest tubes in place
  • Left PICC with tip at cavoatrial junction

In a patient with known pleural effusions / collections, it was suspected that this opacity could represent fluid in the fissure (pleural pseudotumor).

CT was performed and confirmed this. Note the fluid attenuation without solid component on axial. This was within the oblique fissure (see below):


Follow-up chest Xrays demonstrated progressive decrease in size and then disappearance:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pleural Pseudotumor ("fissural pseudomass") refers to loculated fluid in a fissure, mimicking a mass on CXR. Fluid conforms to the fissure, so it often has biconvex/lenticular appearance with tapered ends. CT confirms low attenuation, location, shape, etc.

Why does it happen?

With pleural effusion, lung lobes retract (elastic recoil) which widens the fissure -- creating a potential space that fluid can fill. The RML retracts more (smallest lobe) so the most common location is horizontal (minor) fissure on right.

With pleural effusion Most common location is horizontal fissure (right) but can occur in others.


This most commonly happens in patients with fluid retention (i.e. CHF, cirrhosis, renal disease) -- and will resolve after diuresis. So, CT is not always required if classic -- can just follow up during/after diuresis to resolution.


Don't overcall this either. If the patient doesn't have any pleural effusion or reason to have fluid trapped in the fissure, or it doesn't look classic or not associated with fissural location -- then consider it a mass until proven otherwise!

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 18 - Hampton's Hump (Pulmonary Embolism with Infarct)

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right lower lung wedge shaped opacity extending to pleura (arrows). Notice sparing of the apex of the wedge (medially), giving a rounded appearance [typical of Hampton's hump]
  • Rotated film, limiting assessment of mediastinal and hilar structures
  • No other acute findings

IMPRESSION:

Right lower lobe wedge shaped opacity, suspicious for Hampton's hump (pulmonary infarct due to PE) in this clinical setting. Differential would include infection and other causes of consolidation. Suggest CTPA.


CT confirmed a PE with pulmonary infarct. See the correlate for the infarct below (Hampton's Hump). As an aside notice the 'bubbly consolidation' often seen with infarcts (see CT Chest course):

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Hampton's Hump is one of the two classic signs of PE discussed on CXR. Much more important than the name is understanding what it is, its appearance, and a clinical understanding.

CXR is obviously extremely insensitive for PE, and is most commonly normal in patients with PE. Clinicians worried about PE order a CTPA -- radiologists know this all too well. But, CXR is often performed first to exclude other pathology so you should be aware of the described signs.

Hampton's Hump represents a pulmonary infarct distal to an embolus. Most patients with PE don't have an infarct, so its insensitive. The classic appearance:

  • Pleural-based, wedge-shaped consolidation with base (wide part of triangle) along pleura and apex towards hilum.
  • "Hump" was coined because the apex of the wedge towards the hilum is often spared, and so it is slightly rounded ('hump')
  • Helpful when present (pretty specific if classic like this case), but obviously very insensitive / rarely present in PE


Other PE Signs on CXR:

  • Westermark Sign - regional oligemia (focal paucity of lung vascular markings) distal to acutely occluded pulmonary artery; often peripheral. Rare but specific.
  • Fleischner Sign - prominent / enlarged central PA on CXR. This of course can happen with pulmonary hypertension, but also can be seen with acute enlargement in PE.


Overall, be aware of these signs but know all of them are rare in PE. Again, most commonly CXR is normal in patients with PE. Remember that Hampton's Hump reflects a complication of PE (i.e. pulmonary infarct).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 35 - Interstitial pulmonary edema, Kerley B lines, LA Enlargement

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Enlargement of cardiopericardial silhouette (CTR > 0.5), including left atrial enlargement
  • Vascular redistribution
  • Widening of vascular pedicle
  • Kerley B lines indicating septal thickening / interstitial pulmonary edema.
  • Blunting of the left costophrenic angle, indicating a small pleural effusion.
  • Dilated, air filled esophagus (see lateral posterior to trachea)
  • Sternotomy wires intact

IMPRESSION:

Moderate interstitial pulmonary edema.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
In CHF (Congestive Heart Failure), decreased cardiac output results in dilated pulmonary vessels and then leakage of fluid into the lungs. This case shows interstitial pulmonary edema. Know the findings at each stage (though this is not always linear, it is a helpful way to frame):

Stage 1 (Mild) - Vascular Congestion

  • Upper veins increase in size (similar or larger), often indistinct -- referred to as "vascular redistribution".
  • May also see widened vascular pedicle with fluid overload (see below)
  • Generally no edema in lungs yet

Stage 2 (Moderate) - Interstitial Edema

  • Fluid leaks into interstitial spaces --> interlobular septal thickening ("too many lines" too peripherally on Xray, including Kerley B, as in this case), peribronchial thickening.

Stage 3 (Severe) - Alveolar Edema

  • Fluid eventually leaks into alveoli --> consolidation (confluent, cloud-like opacities). Distribution varies, but often perihilar.
  • Pleural effusions often also present

teachingSections.name: Vascular Pedicle
teachingSections.html:
The Vascular Pedicle is bordered by SVC on the right and subclavian artery on the left. The width is an indicator of intravascular volume.

Measure the pedicle between parallel lines:

  • Right: SVC intersection with R mainstem bronchus
  • Left: takeoff of left subclavian artery from Aorta

It becomes wider with fluid overload in chronic CHF, renal failure, etc. Some rough rules:

  • < 60 mm generally 'normal' on PA
  • >70 mm is a practical threshold to consider volume overload
  • More important is the change compared to prior
  • Concurrent azygos vein dilatation helps support overload

teachingSections.name: Cardiac Chamber Enlargement (Left Atrial)
teachingSections.html:
In addition to identifying cardiomegaly (CTR > 0.5), there are signs of specific chamber enlargement (LA mostly) on Xray that are classic exam questions. Echo, CT, and MRI are obviously much better at this, but Xrays are performed frequently and so it's something you need to know for real life practice too.

Left Atrial Enlargement:

LA sits posteriorly, so on frontal Xray is not normally seen (left heart border is mostly LV, with LA appendage superiorly). With enlargement:

  • Double Density Sign - right side of LA becomes visible either behind RA or beyond right heart border.
  • Most reliable sign: With double density, if measurement from midpoint of left mainstem bronchus to right border of LA is > 7 cm (here about 8.7 cm)
  • Splaying of Carina with bifurcation angle > 90 degrees (here it is > 90 degrees - see image below from this case)

Others described:

  • "Walking man sign" on lateral, with posterior displacement of left mainstem bronchus, which no longer overlaps with right mainstem (see this diagram)

Left Ventricular Enlargement:

  • Left heart border displaced leftward
  • Rounded larger left heart border

Right Ventricular Enlargement:

RV sits anteriorly.

  • Filling of retrosternal space on lateral / greater contact with sternum

Right Atrial Enlargement - less common and harder to delineate on Xray

study.description: XR Chest Routine plus AP Inspiration Portable

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Line overlaying the left hemithorax / peripheral lung (arrows), but with lung markings seen more peripheral to the line (can also see them beyond the line nicely here)

Other Findings:

  • Left sided pacemaker with tip in RV

This case shows a skin fold that mimics the appearance of a pneumothorax. This was suspected by the radiologist and a repeat Xray was performed same day that confirmed no true pneumothorax:

(As an aside, the gas in the RUQ is just gas filled bowel)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We covered pneumothorax in previous cases. This case demonstrates a mimic. While skin folds are usually easy to distinguish from pneumothorax, in this case it is challenging as the morphology of the fold mimics a pleural line.

Remember that you want to see a pleural line with no lung markings beyond it to confidently call it a pneumothorax.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Circumferential nodular pleural thickening
  • Volume loss of the right lung base.
  • Mild enlargement of the cardiac silhouette.

IMPRESSION:

Circumferential nodular pleural thickening is suspicious for malignancy.

In this case, the patient had a history of asbestos exposure and was diagnosted with mesothelioma. The CXR appearance here is classic.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Mesothelioma is an aggressive malignant tumor of mesothelium -- most commonly pleural (but can be peritoneal). It usually occurs long after asbestos exposure (30-40 yrs).

Our case demonstrates the classic appearance: circumferential nodular plaque-like thickening along pleura (often with fissural thickening), with associated volume loss (due to rind surrounding lung). But, may present with:

  • Pleural effusion only (masking disease underlying on CXR)
  • Pleural mass
  • Note CXR generally underestimates extent, and CT much better characterizes.

Although classic for mesothelioma, the appearance of our case with nodular thickening has a broader differential:

  • Pleural metastases (from another primary)
  • Lymphoma
  • Chronic pleural thickening (prior infection, bleeding), TB, other rare entities less common

Pleural plaques seen in minority -- remember, plaques are just a marker of prior asbestos exposure and are generally asymptomatic!


Reminder: Most Important Asbestos-Related Lung Diseases

Pleural Plaques (discussed above):

  • Long latency (>20 yrs)
  • Asymptomatic marker of exposure

Asbestosis (pulmonary fibrosis)

  • Fibrotic lung disease similar to UIP (more severe = parenchymal bands, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing with a lower lobe predominance)
  • Typically 10-15 yrs post-exposure, dose related
  • Pleural plaques may or may not be present

Mesothelioma (cancer)

  • Aggressive malignant tumor of mesothelium (mostly pleural, but can be peritoneal)
  • Very long latency (often 30-40 yrs)
  • Unilateral pleural mass or nodular thickening, can be circumferential and extend into fissures; associated volume loss with progression.

cases.clinicalHistory: r/o lung mets, h/o breast cancer

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Thoracic vertebra appear sclerotic, along with the sternum and manubrium
  • Irregular sclerosis involving several ribs (example arrow).
  • Windowing demonstrates additional areas of bony sclerosis
  • Heart mildly enlarged
  • No lung metastases

IMPRESSION:

Sclerotic lesions in the bones are likely bone metastases. No lung metastases on chest Xray.

CT confirmed these findings:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
CXR is less sensitive than CT for identifying bony metastases. In this case, the pattern of sclerotic metastases is typical of breast cancer (high propensity for bone, especially axial skeleton including manubrium/sternum). Remember to look closely at the bones, especially on the lateral for metastases, compression fractures, etc.

Malignancies that classically produce Sclerotic / Osteoblastic Metastases:

  • Prostate
  • Breast - can be mixed lytic/ sclerotic
  • Others are less common (carcinoid, urothelial, lymphoma especially post-treatment, etc)

Classically lytic (most): RCC, lung, thyroid, melanoma, myeloma, etc)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bilateral pulmonary cavitary lesions (arrows), including right apical, right mid lung, and left upper lung, with other small scattered nodules present

Other Findings:

  • Cardiopericardial silhouette is enlarged
  • No pulmonary edema.
  • No pneumothorax.

IMPRESSION

Bilateral pulmonary cavities. Differential includes septic emboli, cavitary pneumonia, atypical infection and vasculitis.


CT better demonstrates numerous bilateral cavities. Patient had a history of IVDU and tricuspid valve endocarditis. CT correlates for chest CT findings:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Cavitary lesions are gas-filled spaces within a lung mass, nodule, or consolidation. We introduced this in a previous case. This case shows multiple bilateral cavitary lesions, which has unique considerations.


Differential Diagnosis:

Infectious:

  • Septic Emboli - multiple evolving peripheral nodules / cavities, patients often septic. Correlate with any embolic source (endocarditis, IVDU, etc)
  • TB (upper lobe predominant), fungal (see CT chest course)

Noninfectious:

  • GPA (Wegener's) - multiple masses with cavitation
  • Malignancy (cavitary primary or metastases)


CXR Findings to Consider:

  • Distribution (upper/lower, central/peripheral)
  • Number and evolution over time
  • Wall / inner margin (thick/irregular walls can be malignant or aggressive infection; thickness less reliable when multiple nodules)
  • Adenopathy
  • History key (symptoms, primary tumor, presentation, etc)

Recommend CT and correlation with history and laboratory findings re etiology.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Fracture of the right clavicle

Other Findings:

  • Cardiopericardial silhouette and mediastinum within normal limits
  • No focal consolidation or edema.
  • No pleural effusion or pneumothorax
  • Mild biapical pleural thickening (arrows)

IMPRESSION:

Right clavicle fracture. Consider dedicated clavicular Xray.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: We show this case to remind you to look at all of the bones closely in your search pattern. There is an obvious clavicle fracture here. Our MSK ER course will cover clavicular fractures in greater detail.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 90 - Lung Nodule (Nipple Markers confirm not nipple shadow)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Nodular opacity in the right lower lobe (long arrow). On this original routine exam, it was questioned whether this could represent a nipple shadow mimicking a lung nodule.
  • A subsequent exam was performed with nipple markers for this reason, demonstrating the lung nodule separate from the location of the nipple (labeled), confirming a true nodule

Other Findings:

  • Right Lower Lobe Collapse (short arrows). There is volume loss associated with mediastinal shift.
  • Small right pleural effusion

IMPRESSION:

Right lower lobe nodule confirmed. Right lower lobe collapse suspicious for obstructing neoplasm.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We cover lobar collapse and introduce nodules in separate cases. We show this case to introduce nipple shadows on chest Xray, which can mimic a nodule.

For instance, see this image of nipple shadows mimicking nodules. In females, the location can be variable and asymmetric.

In our case, it was questioned whether the RLL nodule was a true nodule or a nipple shadow. A repeat Xray was performed with nipple markers (radiopaque) to help determine if this was the case. In this case, the nodule is separate from the nipple -- and was therefore a true nodule. This was a metastasis.

cases.clinicalHistory: Short of breath. Prior CXR normal.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
On Portable Erect Xray:

  • Apparent elevation of the right hemidiaphragm (right dome is 6-7 cm above the left, normally < 3 cm). This was new compared to prior.
  • Compare appearance with prior subpulmonic effusion - notice the shape of the diaphragm and visible lung behind the elevated diaphragm (with some subsegmental atelectasis).

IMPRESSION:

Apparent elevation of the right hemidiaphragm on this AP view.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
On normal PA films, the right hemidiaphragm sits about 1 intercostal space above the left (< 3 cm). So, considered abnormal on PA when:

  • Left is higher than the right
  • Right > 3 cm above the left

For AP/Portable films, inspiratory depth is often suboptimal and there may be more rotation, which make a particular measurement less reliable -- and overcalling common. Still, the finding should be noted as suspected or apparent elevation (as was noted in this case). This can be confirmed thereafter with an erect PA or other imaging if/when clinically relevant.

Causes of elevated hemidiaphragm:

  • Diaphragm:
  • **Phrenic nerve palsy - this is the primary concern, often post-op, neurological, or idiopathic. Will see paradoxical motion on U/S sniff test.
  • Other neurological cause
  • Eventration mimic
  • Subdiaphragmatic irritation or mass effect, e.g.:
  • Subphrenic collection
  • Liver (tumor, enlarged)
  • Above Diaphragm:
  • Significant atelectasis / collapse
  • Prior surgery / lobectomy
  • Fibrosis
  • Mimics:
  • Subpulmonic effusion (prior case)
  • Other pleural abnormality (i.e. tumor)
  • Diaphragmatic hernia or rupture (trauma)

Practical Approach:

  1. Confirm its real (good inspiration PA, nonrotated) - if clearly elevated, note if new or present prior
  2. Look for obvious cause: known subdiaphragmatic cause? Lung collapse/significant atelectasis? Prior surgery or predisposing factor?
  3. Further evaluation if required: i.e. sniff test if concerned re palsy; CT if concern for other cause (hernia, rupture, mass, etc)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left lower lobe retrocardiac nodule, also seen on lateral

Other Findings:

  • Right pleural thickening laterally
  • Right lung demonstrates increased reticulation and peripheral opacities, related to known chronic infections (was improved compared to prior study) and reticular opacities seen on prior CTs (not the reason for showing this case)
  • Left pectoral pacemaker with single lead in the RV
  • Prior mitral and tricuspid repair (frontal, lateral)
  • Sternotomy wires intact

IMPRESSION:

Retrocardiac nodule.

Right sided lung and pleural changes improved from prior (prior not shown).


CT better depicts the retrocardiac nodule:

The nodule contained popcorn calcification and fat, characterized as a hamartoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: We introduce solitary pulmonary nodule separately. We show this case to demonstrate a common blindspot on CXR - the retrocardiac region. This is a review area. Window the image to check behind the heart.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 91 - Lymphoma (anterior mediastinal mass, paratracheal adenopathy, lung nodule)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Abnormal soft tissue in the anterior mediastinum with a focal mass along the left mediastinum the region of the AP window which measures 6 cm. Note loss of retrosternal clear space on lateral.
  • Widening of the right paratracheal stripe (arrows), measures up to 1.9 cm.
  • Nodule at the right base which measures 12 mm and another smaller nodule more inferiorly in the right lateral costophrenic sulcus.
  • No pleural effusion

IMPRESSION:

Anterior mediastinal mass and lymphadenopathy with suspected pulmonary nodules. Lymphoma would be the primary consideration, with germ cell tumors and thymic neoplasms also possible. CT chest suggested.


CT confirmed anterior mediastinal mass, adenopathy, and lung nodules (both confirmed on CT). This was Hodgkin's Lymphoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case shows an anterior mediastinal mass, right paratracheal thickening, and lung nodules in the setting of lymphoma. We introduce each of these CXR findings separately (including normal paratracheal stripe, localizing mediastinal masses, and lung nodules). Here, you must make multiple findings to consider lymphoma first, which was confirmed thereafter.


Lymphoma is malignant neoplasms of lymphocytes. Two broad groups referred to are:

  • Hodgkin Lymphoma - about 10%, defined by Reed-Sternberg cells
  • Non-Hodgkin Lymphomas - heterogeneous B/T/NK cell neoplasms (90%)

Diagnosis is made based on tissue, but typical distributions:

  • HL: predominantly nodal, cervical / mediastinal (anterior mediastinal common), often 'contiguous' spread, intrathoracic nodal disease more common than NHL
  • NHL: more likely to present in extranodal sites (head/neck mucosal, GI tract, skin, bone, others). Nodal disease also common but less predictable / often non-contiguous. Mediastinal dominance less typical.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Complete collapse of the right upper lobe (total airlessness/atelectasis), with a reverse golden S sign -- which refers to the "S" shaped margin of the collapsed lung (arrows).
  • Right hemidiaphragm is elevated with juxtaphrenic peak (due to volume loss)

Other Findings:

  • Heart size is normal.
  • Calcifications in the aortic arch

IMPRESSION:

Appearance likely due to an obstructing carcinoma involving right upper lobe bronchus. CT suggested.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
The Reverse Golden S sign is another one that you must know for exams and real life radiology.

Most commonly, right upper lobe collapse results in collapse in a superomedial direction that produces a linear margin. In contrast, in the reverse golden S sign the upper lung is pulled medially, but there is convex bulge inferiorly (circle) due to neoplasm/mass with positive mass effect. This results in the margin looking like an S (a reverse S) - hence the name of the sign.

The sign indicates an obstructing mass, usually primary lung cancer (but could be a met or other neoplasm), with collapse of the right upper lobe. CT should be performed for further assessment.

Related Links:

  • Here is a nice case review of the reverse golden S sign in Radiology.
  • For interest, here's the original paper describing this by Golden in AJR in 1924!!

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 106 - Vagus Nerve Stimulator, No Acute Findings

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Vagus nerve stimulator device. Pulse generator seen clearly. Zoom in and window to see the leads and the electrodes (described in learning material, must zoom quite a bit)

Other Findings:

  • Cardiac and mediastinal contours are normal.
  • The lungs are clear with no consolidation.
  • No pneumothorax or pleural effusion.

IMPRESSION:

No acute findings.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is another normal Xray to keep you honest. But, we also show you another device that you will come across.


A Vagus Nerve Stimulator (VNS) is an implantable device used to treat a range of conditions, including epilepsy and depression. Exact mechanism is unknown but stimulation of vagus nerve leads to suppression of neuronal activity in certain areas.

The VNS consists of:

  • Subcutaneous pulse generator in the left infraclavicular region
  • Two leads tunneled to the vagus nerve in the lower neck (left aspect of lower cervical spine)
  • Electrodes - two small metallic ribbons, one superior to the other, representing the negative and positive electrodes (straddle the vagus nerve)

**ZOOM IN to upper images to see labels and fine details of leads and electrodes here.


Complications:

  • Pneumothorax
  • Lead and/or electrode breakage or displacement -- which can be assessed on Xray. Here's an example where the electrode became disconnected from the wire/lead.
  • Hoarseness (surgical injury, or due to stimulation itself)
  • Would infection, hematoma (CT or U/S may see)

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 14 - Pectus Excavatum with heart displacement

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Indistinct right heart border
  • Leftward shift of the heart
  • Vertically angulated ribs
  • Lateral: chest wall abnormality with sternal depression

Other Findings:

  • Mild enlargement of the transverse diameter of cardiopericardial silhouette.
  • Elongated chest with hyperinflation (> 11 posterior ribs seen here)
  • No consolidation. No findings of pneumonia. No other acute findings.

IMPRESSION:

Pectus excavatum with leftward shift of the cardiovascular silhouette. Likely underlying Marfan's syndrome.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pectus Excavatum is a congenital anterior chest wall deformity with posterior depression of sternum. It's the most common pectus deformity and associated with connective tissue disorders (including Marfan's, like in this case).

CXR Appearance:

  • Indistinct or obscured right heart border, apparent leftward cardiac displacement
  • Slanted or vertical ribs
  • Lateral view: depressed sternum filling retrosternal airspace

Severity often quantified at CT (Haller Index = transverse / AP diameter of chest (> 3.25 = considered severe).


Why it matters?

  • Sternal depression can cause cardiac compression, displacement, and reduced lung volumes --> symptoms
  • PA CXR findings can mimic a RML consolidation (silhouette), so need to know the typical appearance here!
  • Repair for severe cases


Pectus Carinatum - anterior sternal protrusion with increased AP diameter. Lateral chest wall flattening. Often associated with scoliosis.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left upper lobe cavity with rounded mass within, producing a surrounding air-crescent ("air-crescent" sign / previously called Monod sign). Zoom in to this one.
  • Cavity is thin walled, with some opacity / thickening along superior margin

IMPRESSION:

Left upper lobe fungus ball (mycetoma).


CT performed also shows this nicely:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Mycetoma ("Fungus Ball") refers to a fungal mass colonizing a pre-existing cavity. It can be caused by multiple fungi, but since aspergillus is most common, aspergilloma is often used synonymously. This case demonstrates the classic appearance of an aspergilloma. You should know what it is, the classic sign, and understand / distinguish between the various forms of 'pulmonary aspergillosis'.

Aspergilloma:

  • Non-invasive fungal ball that fills a pre-existing cavity (cavity is there for another reason like prior TB, sarcoid, bulla, bronchiectasis).
  • Often asymptomatic, but can be symptomatic (hemoptysis, cough)

Classic Appearance:

  • Upper Lobe Cavity containing rounded soft tissue mass with surrounding air-crescent -- shown in this case but better seen on CT.
  • "Air Crescent" is preferred over the old name (Monod Sign).
  • Fungus ball is often mobile

teachingSections.name: Pulmonary Aspergillosis
teachingSections.html:
Pulmonary Aspergillosis is a broad term referring to conditions caused by Aspergillus. Keep the broad categories (classic forms), and their classic appearances on imaging, straight. These come up again and again so you need to know this! We introduce the main concept below:

Aspergilloma:

  • This case
  • A non-invasive fungal ball in a pre-existing cavity. Minimal symptoms usually.

Invasive Aspergillosis - more severe infection in the immunocompromised, broken down into:

  • Angioinvasive Aspergillosis - most severe and aggressive form that you need to know, in severely immunocompromised patients (life threatening). Invasion into pulmonary arteries leads to areas of lung necrosis and hemorrhage. Classic appearance is nodules with surrounding ground glass due to a halo of hemorrhage. Importantly, after recovery the necrotic lung can separate from surrounding parenchyma, also giving an air crescent appearance! Thus, air-crescent can be seen in either healing angioinvasive aspergillosis OR aspergilloma -- but the clinical situation (symptomatic, treated, multifocal, sick) and appearance is different.
  • Airway Invasive Aspergillosis - invasive, involves airways, variable appearance from nodules to tree-in-bud to bronchopneumonia (looks like any other bronchopneumonia). Less severe and less specific features on imaging than angioinvasive.
  • Subacute invasive aspergillosis - previously "semi-invasive" - subacute to chronic form of invasive aspergillosis. Less important to know for now and beyond scope here.

ABPA - Hypersensitivity to aspergillus in the airways --> bronchiectasis and mucoid impaction (finger-in-glove) classic. Discussed separately.


This will help frame it:

  • ABPA = hyperimmune response
  • Invasive Aspergillosis = immunocompromised
  • Aspergilloma = fungus ball in a cavity there for other reasons

"Air Crescent":

Use Air Crescent for BOTH of the following (based on 2024 Fleischner):

  • Aspergilloma (prev "Monod")
  • Healing angioinvasive aspergillosis (retracted necrotic lung)

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 8 - Collapse of medial left lower lobe

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Triangular opacity in the posterior medial aspect of the left lung (retrocardiac sail sign) and some hazy opacification projected in the posterior lung on the lateral radiograph (should get progressively darker inferiorly). Findings are in keeping with partial collapse left lower lobe (was seen on recent CT)
  • Remainder of the lungs are clear.
  • No significant pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

CT correlate for the finding:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
Left Lower Lobe Collapse appears as a triangular / wedge opacity from left hilum and inferiorly, seen behind the heart. In this case there is collapse of the medial left lower lobe that was better seen at CT rather than complete lobar collapse, making the findings less pronounced on the lateral.

Other findings with lobar collapse:

  • Associated volume loss (crowding in lower lobe, compensatory hyperinflation left upper lobe)
  • On lateral, increased posteroinferior opacity with silhouetting of the diaphragm. In our case, the density is slightly higher than would be expected over the inferior spine as a result.
